1/ LLM providers aren't necessarily the new tech giants with all the power—what if they're just "dumb pipes," like cell networks? The real leverage might go to whoever crafts the best user experiences on top of these models, not the model creators themselves. (1/n)

2/ The "killer app" for LLMs might not be a chat interface. If something more creative or collaborative emerges, third-party tools could easily eclipse the default 1P offerings. The early winners may not hold on as the space matures and use cases explode. (2/n)

3/ In AI's early days, curiosity & experimentation matter more than hustle. You succeed not by grinding out repetitive work but by inventing and playing, actively seeking surprises & new angles. Let LLMs multiply your agency—not shrink it. (3/n)

4/ LLMs can reflect cross-disciplinary insights, but only if you ask interesting questions. The gap between what these tools can do and what typical users get from them is mostly about user curiosity & creativity, not model quality. (4/n)

5/ The "default advantage" doesn't guarantee compounding power for LLM providers. Unlike Google Search, OpenAI no longer has the top model or a strong network effect from its user data. The aggregator playbook might not apply here. (5/n)

6/ Beware: If you optimize solely for engagement (what users want right now), you get algo-slop. The future should be "meaning-maximizing" computing—software that helps us become our best selves, not just chase dopamine hits. (6/n)

7/ Content creators: optimize for what *you* find meaningful—not for the algorithm, not for virality. The best predictor of success is sticking with authentic work you love, and you never know how many others will resonate with your niche. (7/n)

8/ The shift from engagement to meaning will open space for more personal, bespoke software—features for niche groups, not features built only for mass appeal. Imagine a world where software flocks to users, not the other way around. (8/n)

9/ Agency is a muscle. AIs can boost it, but if we cede too much, we risk losing touch with our ability to choose. Delegating to AI must be intentional—otherwise, we end up like inheritors of wealth who never learned to steer their own ship. (9/n)

10/ In a world swirling with hyper-optimized, algorithmic content, staying connected to your own voice and creating space for creativity is a true act of resistance—and a path to meaning. (10/n)

1/17 At Google, our strength was making Search Quality a true center of excellence. The core of your value should be the thing you pour most of your energy into—perfection everywhere else is a waste. Focus on your differentiator. The rest just needs to be "good enough."

2/17 Abduction—yes, the Sherlock Holmes kind—is my secret weapon. Unlike induction & deduction, abduction helps you create hypotheses out of weird details. It’s about betting on what *might* be true, then running experiments and learning from real-world feedback.

3/17 Want to find your next big idea? Watch your most passionate users, especially when their behavior seems bizarre. Odds are, they see something hidden. Steelman their actions: what if their pain is more widespread but only they are tough enough to crawl through the broken glass?

4/17 Betting on abducted hypotheses sometimes gives you higher returns than expected. Build no-brainer features that could serve surprising new user segments. Worst case? You create value you already predicted. Best case? You unlock something much bigger.

5/17 Here’s the unpopular truth: novel ideas usually don’t work. But if you ever see a game-changing result, you can bet a novel idea is behind it.

6/17 The design of our tools shapes our behaviors. Google Docs’ detailed comments are great for nitpicking, but sometimes broad, synthesized points get lost in the noise. Don’t confuse lots of detail with genuinely useful feedback.

7/17 A/B testing is powerful, but beware: it can turn into p-hacking on steroids and amplify Goodhart’s law. If you measure only some outcomes, the others (externalities!) can quietly accumulate until they swamp your metrics.

8/17 True perfection is a mirage. Chasing the illusion as you scale will make you miserable—better to get the fundamentals right and accept that optics will get messy.

9/17 The world of atoms requires effort to move anything. The world of bits (software, data) is just easier, which flatters our egos in tech. Don’t let it go to your head.

10/17 Org problems beyond a certain scale are always systems problems, not personal drama. Frame them as such: it’s way more interesting and less draining.

11/17 Amazon’s 70% rule: make most decisions when you’re 70% sure. Gathering more info gets ever more costly, with diminishing returns. If your feedback is just, “Let’s be extra sure,” you might be subtracting value.

12/17 Rigidity = efficiency, but adaptation is survival. Software’s fast pace layer means rigid orgs die quickly—adapt or be outpaced.

13/17 Most hard things are hard for boring reasons. Don’t overthink it.

14/17 Intrinsic motivation is essential for ambiguous problem spaces. You can’t manufacture excitement—find people who have the fire already, don’t try to ignite it where there’s nothing to burn.

15/17 It’s so easy to copy the visible stuff—spreadsheets, processes—but what matters is the invisible strategic clarity beneath. Mimicking the surface just gets you a high school play, not the real thing.

16/17 Passing through filters never feels like winning; failing is loud, success is mostly invisible. Survivorship bias is real—don’t forget luck’s role in your wins.

17/17 Best ideas came from time set aside for thinking, not meetings. You’ll never “have” the time for synthesis—you have to make it. Guard it like treasure.

1/🧵 Most orgs blame failed strategies on bad execution, not the strategy itself. But many things are harder to pull off than they look, and coordination headwinds are *massive*.

2/ Always ask: if the strategy were wrong, what would it look like? If reality matches, maybe it’s the strategy, not just execution.

3/ Clean execution ≠ correct execution. Orgs select for cleanliness because it’s visible, but real-world correctness is often messy.

4/ Platforms ≠ apps. An app is a single bet; a platform is a meta-strategy, a swarm of bets that can only prove value as unplanned wins emerge over time.

5/ You can't build a platform alone—you coevolve with your ecosystem. Most iconic platforms started as products, found PMF, then opened up.

6/ Killer use cases for platforms only exist in retrospect. If you know what will work up front, you’re building an app, not a platform.

7/ Apple's ATT policy was a tectonic shift. Beyond hurting Facebook, it decimated countless long-tail businesses—second-order effects that get overlooked.

8/ Evolution is great for the collective but harsh for individuals. Collective growth comes from individuals locked in brutal, competitive churn.

9/ In high-skill domains (like startups), who wins is mostly luck—after filtering for top ability. Pooling outcomes could protect downside & unleash more innovation.

10/ Tech shifts—like the move from steam to electric power—aren’t just about swapping components. New paradigms unlock totally new possibilities. Don’t retrofit, reinvent!

11/ The app economy’s monoliths optimize for the lowest common denominator. Tons of features serving small passionate groups never get built. What if we atomized experiences into swarms of self-assembling micro-apps?

12/ LLMs are not perfect engines—they’re like impossibly precocious middle-schoolers. Treat them as intelligence amps (IA), not moral agents. Keep feedback loops short, like agile dev, so they don’t get lost.

13/ Kayfabe at work is real. Quitting lets you drop the act—it’s like flying. Let’s spend less time pretending and more on what actually matters.

1/ AI & Philosophy at Oxford: My talk was all about harnessing swarm dynamics—letting go of control and "surfing" collective energy to unlock outcomes you can't direct. Small, consistent nudges can move the whole swarm without anyone noticing!

2/ Swarms act like flood-fills in possibility space. One plan = sublinear results, but a swarm = linear scaling with investment. Induce a tiny, consistent bias and eventually you get massive, emergent direction—with each participant barely noticing the influence.

3/ It’s like playing macro-scale Maxwell’s Demon: carve out a subset already heading the way you want, watch self-acceleration kick in, and soon, a whole movement snowballs. Just a little judgment creates surprising, self-fueling force.

4/ Emergent systems—think termite mounds or even TikTok sea shanties—show magical, cathedral-scale results with no central planner. The narrative comes after: it’s all post-hoc stories over a sea of forgotten failed experiments.

5/ These ideas aren't just theoretical. I see them in tech (aggregators, LLMs, platform dynamics) and even personal development. Ride the wave breaking where you are, not where you wish it was, and embrace the swarm’s asymmetric power!

1/ Apple's new AI: not disruptive, but a "sustaining innovation" that tightens its grip. By making apps legible for automation (what I call "fracking"), Apple keeps adding seams, squeezing apps to work inside its flows, building on a decade-old playbook.

2/ This approach delivers user value but hits a privacy & distribution ceiling—it can't run apps you haven't installed or avoid plausible creepy outcomes. At best, you get "accelerated copy paste," not transformative change.

3/ The alternatives, like "over-the-top" automation (Arc Browser, Multi-ON, etc), take a different tack: wide but shallow integration, working with unmodified apps. Both models have limits, but neither is truly revolutionary—yet.

4/ The REAL shift? Private & confidential compute. Apple's Private Cloud Compute locks service authors out; confidential compute locks cloud hosts out. The gold standard: both at once, but tough for incumbents to retrofit.

5/ This is an underappreciated sea change: consumers will expect privacy previously seen only in defense contracts. Cloud computing is finally moving from landlord/renter dynamics to embassy-like sovereignty for users. That shift is inevitable!

1/ The web platform is evolving fast, unlocking wild new capabilities! Did you know you can access your webcam directly in the browser with just a bit of code? I made a live photo booth app—rotating my face in 3D, adding sepia filters, all in real-time. 🔥

2/ Audio on the web used to mean clunky Flash, but not anymore. With new Web Audio APIs, you can generate & manipulate sound live—think games with perfect timing, collaborative music straight from your browser, and visualizations that react to the beat.

3/ Real-time collaboration is now super easy. Chat updates instantly, drag-n-drop music syncs playback for everyone, and custom avatars can actually dance to the music using audio analysis—all natively, plugin-free, on the web!

4/ 3D scenes and serious physics? No problem. With JavaScript and WebGL, I made complex worlds with ripples, lighting, and water—all in-browser. Want to port a native game to the web? Native Client lets you run legacy codebases, even using wireless controllers, seamlessly.

5/ The future of the web is mind-blowing: real-time video, audio, multiplayer collaboration, stunning 3D, plug-and-play game ports—all running securely in the browser. Can’t wait to see what’s next! 🚀

2/ I see "platform" as a *lens*: it helps reveal interactions, power dynamics, & coordination challenges between agents. Marketplace? Just a special type. Multi-layered, self-reinforcing, it's platforms all the way down! #SystemsThinking

3/ The real power of a platform? The *swarm*—users, developers, customers acting independently. You only need one member to find a breakthrough. Swarms create unpredictable, outsized value. #Emergence #SwarmIntelligence

4/ Agency matters. Every player—employee or developer—has choices, big or small. Don't let anyone tell you employees are powerless or not "customers" of your business ecosystem. Agency changes the game! #WorkplaceAgency

5/ Community-building is core. Secret, high-trust "gardens" let new ideas thrive, free from performative seriousness. Regularly trickling in new perspectives keeps groupthink at bay and innovation alive. #CommunityDesign

6/ True creativity is fundamentally playful. That irreverent, undisciplined approach? It's where connections form and wild ideas are born. Play > performative seriousness, every time. #Playfulness #Innovation

7/ Control vs. ubiquity is *the* trade-off. Want a huge, generative ecosystem? You have to let go of tight control, including of IP. Mixing, remixing, and cross-pollinating ideas is where the magic happens—and where value emerges. #EcosystemStrategy

8/ Infinite games—like robust communities—generate meaning for their members and unexpected business value for organizations. Focus on creating flexible gardens and watch value propagate in wild, new ways. #InfiniteGame #Leadership

1/21 ELI5 docs aren’t just for kids—they force ruthless clarity. Distill complex ideas into simple caricatures, and suddenly everyone has a backbone to hang details on. Clarity isn't dumbing down; it’s power.

2/21 Flywheels (compounding positive loops) take time. If you judge progress too soon, you'll kill the momentum before it starts. Set goals and timelines modest at the start, or you’ll default to linear, non-compounding strategies.

3/21 Tradeoffs aren’t about “either/or.” Both ends are valuable! Always maintain a balanced stance—lean one way for context, but never lose touch with the other side or you’ll misjudge when things tip.

4/21 Most metrics are for satisficing, not maximizing. Clear the bar, stop optimizing. Maximizing past the “green zone” creates huge opportunity costs. Only sober minds can set those bars—never during crisis.

5/21 Perfect plans? Impossible in a world of uncertainty. Real meta-planning assumes chaos and builds resilience, not rigidity.

6/21 Big, concrete ideas can *feel* abstract just because there’s no handle to grab onto. Build small, concrete touchpoints to make them real.

7/21 Special? Sure, to yourself. But globally? Only if you convince uninterested outsiders. Narrative matters more than you think.

8/21 APIs are like ecosystems, not artifacts. Ship something decent, then evolve it based on real-world feedback. No one gets it perfect in a vacuum.

9/21 Prioritization is always in context. You can’t decide what matters in isolation—you always balance tradeoffs. The right question is, “Do we care enough about X to *not* do Y?”

10/21 "Every system is perfectly designed to get the results it gets."—Deming. Machines repeat, living things adapt. The power is in agency.

11/21 Surprise failures? Don’t just shrug—dig into why your prediction failed. Invest effort proportionally to the surprise. That’s how you learn.

12/21 Orgs coordinate best around obvious answers. When there’s no single silver bullet, progress is inch-by-inch until critical mass is hit.

13/21 For privacy, systems should show what’s being saved *as it happens*. Anything less creates nasty surprises. OSes do this; AI assistants mostly don’t.

14/21 Accepting 20% more chaos = 200% more output—under the right conditions.

15/21 Disruption for incumbents isn’t about ignorance. Even when they see the threat, there are no good, risk-free moves—so inertia wins. Virality always beats lumbering giants.

16/21 In big orgs, agility & velocity are at odds. Your local speed is someone else’s chaos. Global fast means local patience.

17/21 Boundary Objects are tools/info used and understood differently by different groups, creating shared meaning across silos. Vital for coordination.

18/21 If something is alive—like a meme or scenius—it escapes its creator’s control. That loss of control is where emergent value comes in. The mess *is* the point.

19/21 My two key reminders: 1) Compassion is strength. 2) Your need for certainty is holding you back.

20/21 In a noisy world, taste cuts through. Having taste is a liability in big orgs but a superpower everywhere else.

21/21 For the next AI era, cryptographic signatures are our only real lever for preserving trust. AI fakes almost everything except that.

1/ Building products for every use case seems efficient—just extend what you have, right? But watch out: complexity doesn't grow linearly, it's combinatorial. Each new feature makes every other one harder to reason about and maintain. 

2/ Eventually, you get a product so tangled that every new extension feels like wading through mud. Avoid this by building with open-ended primitives—think legos, not hyper-specific lego sets. It’s algorithm-hard up front, but pays off in flexibility.

3/ When you charge users a marginal price for marginal usage, they naturally consume less. Ignore this, and you risk giving away more value than you capture—especially true for products built on LLM features, where marginal cost isn't trivial!

4/ Fully automated AI can give you golden turds: answers that seem great but are subtly wrong. The antidote? Inline diffs and human-AI iteration, not letting the LLM wander off alone. Some domains can't afford gilded mistakes.

5/ Products are much more than the code: the ecosystem multiplies their value. Growth happens when a service's community builds on it, not just the original creators. Designing for ecosystem compounding is high leverage.

6/ If your system passes tough decisions to users without giving them actual agency, it's not real transparency—it's abdication. Design for agency, not just for exposure of controls. Make the default safe and easy, don't punt the hard part!

7/ Ecosystems thrive when drawn to heat, not light. People motivated by the substance and energy (the heat) make the system better for everyone, while those chasing buzz (the light) don't sustain value. Be open, yet illegible: attract the truly engaged.

8/ A local-first, privacy-centered approach isn't just better for security—it’s better for longevity. If a cloud service dies, there goes your data. Local-first means your data outlasts anyone's business model.

9/ Most tools force users to pick a schema upfront, but real creation is messier. The magic of LLMs is in letting users stay fuzzy and gradual—collect, bucket, and only structure when ready. Let humans act like humans, and add structure as needed.

1/ AI's real center of gravity isn’t the model—it's *context*. Whoever owns the context layer shapes everything. Handing both to one entity is a recipe for dangerous power concentration. Think: "One app to rule them all." We need new technical paradigms fast. 

2/ The sharpest risk isn't just prompt injection or model vulnerabilities. It's the emergence of vertical integration: context + model + omniscient aggregator. That creates a hyper-powerful gatekeeper with perfect recall of you—and everyone else. No precedent for that scale.

3/ The memory feature in LLMs feels like a black box to users. We’re not given the real knobs. Companies default to "engagement maxing" and restrict true user control—not out of malice, but because the gravity of incentives always wins.

4/ We talk about “users,” but isn’t that just commercial language for humans? What if we designed systems for people, not for engagement? Value is what machines see. *Values* are about our intentional aspirations as humans. We need a shift.

5/ Want real progress? Attach policies to *data*, not just code. The app model is outdated for the era of infinite AI-powered software. When data defines what code can do, you unlock freedom and privacy—without having to trust every creator forever.

1/7 People often say I have great self-control, but honestly? My brain is a master at making excuses. Put donuts in front of me and I'll find a reason to eat them all. 🍩😂

2/7 My "trick" for self-control is simple: avoid giving my brain a chance to negotiate. When it comes to habits, "always" rules are WAY better than "sometimes" ones.

3/7 Years ago, surrounded by free snacks at Google, I gained 20+ lbs thanks to endless peanut M&Ms. My solution? I created an unwavering rule: I would ALWAYS go to the gym every day. No matter what.

4/7 The magic wasn't in tough workouts, but in the consistency. No debate, no excuses. Even on off days, I'd go just to sit on a bike for 20 minutes. The hardest part was removed: deciding IF I would go.

5/7 The longer I stuck to my always rule, the easier it got. Breaking the streak started to feel wasteful. Willpower barely needed!

6/7 Life isn’t simple though. Travel threatened my streak. Instead of becoming flexible (and losing the magic), I SYSTEMIZED exceptions: the rule became, “always work out daily, unless it’s the first/last day of a trip.”

7/7 The key? Make the rule clear BEFORE excuses creep in. That’s how I lost the weight and built habits that last—despite being terrible at self-control. Always rules > sometimes rules, every time.

1/15 Never scar on the first cut: If you design a fix around a one-off problem, you'll overfit and make systems too rigid. You need multiple data points to see patterns and build flexible, systemic solutions. #work #systems

2/15 Risk is weird: Most failures are small and self-correcting, but there’s always a hidden threshold where everything can break. Tail risk and black swans are real — in complex systems, the improbable is inevitable. #risk #complexity

3/15 Everyone’s constantly making bets, even when they think they aren’t. The key is making hypotheses explicit, not relying only on gut. Written ideas attract disconfirming evidence and help us make smarter bets. #decisionmaking

4/15 Machines (including orgs/systems) can’t truly change themselves—they just get more efficient at what they already do. Real change comes from those with empathy for the system, standing one foot inside, one outside. #orgchange

5/15 Partnerships: The partner who doesn't worry at night is the one with real power. Don’t fool yourself with "pockets of leverage"—it’s the sum total that counts. #powerdynamics

6/15 You can't excel at changing a system you don’t understand deeply. Mastery comes first, then you earn the right to improve it—otherwise, you risk breaking load-bearing walls you don’t see. #expertise

7/15 Build from the inside out. Don’t make a ship in a bottle and expect it to survive at sea. Bootstrap solutions in real environments so they’re proven effective at every stage. #productdev

8/15 Heroic jerks sometimes change the world—but most fail quietly. Their real superpower is lack of self-doubt, but selection bias makes us overrate their importance. #leadership

9/15 Compassionate system thinkers can create immense value out of uninspiring situations—but they often look kooky and rarely get credit. Alchemy isn’t visible until it works. #systems #value

10/15 Judo moves are subtle tweaks that radically shift trajectories, but to outsiders, they look like luck. Repeated success and peer acknowledgment can help distinguish skill from chance. #change

11/15 Resentment disengages. Engagement powers curiosity, and curiosity fuels learning. #culture

12/15 Cohesion requires some sacrifice of individual agency. Over-emphasizing "visionary" leadership can breed chaos and thrash. #teams

13/15 In open source, control goes to whoever invests most, not just the creator. Once success is visible, capitalized entities can take over. Not sure there’s a fix for that. #opensource

14/15 Special treatment? Others must see your claim as special, not just you. Walk in their shoes before asking. #empathy

15/15 Your place in a system determines your view. At the top, privilege is invisible. At the bottom, the system feels oppressive. Combine perspectives for the full picture. #systems

1/16 Deciding what to build is easier with fewer people—alignment effort scales with the square of the group size! But fewer minds means fewer game-changing ideas or critical feedback. Trade-offs everywhere.

2/16 As something succeeds and grows, it inevitably becomes more bland. Every improvement risks undoing prior work, so the system gets tangled up in vetoes and constraints. From the outside, it just looks like stagnation.

3/16 Consensus swarms lead to blandness, while anti-consensus swarms create novelty through internal competition and differentiation. Organizations lie on a spectrum here, and where you are determines your fate.

4/16 Everyone hunts for silver bullets—big, obvious unlocks—but they're rare. More often, progress comes from silver swarms: many small, decentralized ideas that combine to shift the whole system. Easy to miss if you aren’t looking for it.

5/16 Swarms discover use cases faster than big monoliths—if they could have secure access to user data! Today’s privacy paradigm keeps swarms out, so slow, bland aggregators dominate. What if the laws of physics allowed privacy-safe swarms?

6/16 Most assume AI will centralize into mega-apps, but maybe the future isn’t "apps" at all. Imagine a swarm of microservices, each with its own edge, working together in a post-app ecosystem.

7/16 The more successful your product, the more you’re subject to the "Tyranny of the Marginal User"—trying to please everyone leads to a drab, lowest-common-denominator outcome.

8/16 Money simplifies and measures value, but in doing so, it can hollow out meaning. Marrying for money? You’ll keep paying for it, emotionally and spiritually.

9/16 Communities that grow slowly—forcing members through a gauntlet—build resilience and quality. Quick booms fill up with low-engagement users who dumb things down. Scarcity breeds depth.

10/16 We try to make LLMs act like normal computers, but their magic is in being "squishy"—embrace the weirdness! Don’t force new things to look like the old.

11/16 Platform thinking is hard because you need to design for emergence, not just top-down results. Magic happens when you enable bottom-up innovation—in products, organizations, & ecosystems.

12/16 Data is viral and non-rivalrous. Once someone sees your data, it’s out there forever. Fixing privacy means letting third parties help *without* ever seeing your data—an unsolved but critical challenge.

13/16 Perfect security is a myth—it gets infinitely expensive. Threats evolve, so security must co-evolve. Focus on incremental improvements, not absolutes.

14/16 Compounding loops (growth) are auto-balanced by opposing loops (resource constraints, competition, etc.). Beware projecting trends infinitely—balancing forces always kick in.

1/🧵 Ever notice how Microsoft thrives through tech eras? It’s all about the suite. The suite is the star, not any one product—constantly evolving, modular, and always making sense internally. That’s resilience by design. (1/8)

4/ The same logic applies in organizations. The bigger you get, the more you fight the “coordination headwind”—problems aren’t boulders, they’re swarms. Just adding people doesn’t help; coordination costs explode. (4/8)

5/ It’s like the rocket equation for companies: Add more mass, need more fuel. Add more people, need exponentially more coordination. That’s why mega-orgs can become kafka-esque and slow. (5/8)

6/ Maybe AI can shake this up by giving small teams tons of leverage, breaking the cycle. But beware: structure trumps simple headcount if you want to scale output without drowning in coordination costs. (6/8)

7/ When building products, consider the context and suite. Interdependent bets are valuable together—VC “blockbuster-or-bust” logic fails inside a suite. Treat the whole as more than the sum of parts. (7/8)

8/ tl;dr: Build suites, not single products. Embrace modularity, combat coordination drag, and stay adaptable. That’s the playbook for surviving tech eras and scaling organizations. (8/8)

1/🧵 Protocol vs Platform: Here’s how I see the debate. Using a platform means relying on someone else’s code—whether that’s open source or a service/APIs under their control. You don’t run the show. (1/7)

2/ A protocol, though, is just a rulebook for communication—no obligation to use anyone’s code. You can always write your own. That’s pure agency. (2/7)

3/ Sometimes one platform gets huge, and its API *becomes* the protocol—even if that wasn’t the original intent. First-mover gravity is real. (3/7)

4/ In my writing, I sometimes call what others would name a “protocol” a “platform”—the line is blurry in practice, especially as ecosystems evolve and API dominance grows. (4/7)

5/ Open systems aren’t about source code or APIs—they’re about configurability and whether users can route around any component. True openness is about *user agency* at the layer closest to them. (5/7)

6/ Protocols and platforms morph, intermingle, and sometimes become inseparable. But if you want real freedom or resilience, lean into protocols—make routing and combinability easy. (6/7)

7/ tl;dr: Openness lives where the user sits. The protocol/platform distinction matters less than giving people true agency. That’s where innovation happens. (7/7)

1/ Openness isn't binary—it's a spectrum. Systems aren't simply open or closed; how open they are depends on real power dynamics between entities. If one player goes rogue, can the ecosystem recover? That's the real test. #Openness #Ecosystems

2/ We tend to see our own motives as good, but if you swapped your name for your fiercest competitor in your ecosystem, would you trust them? This empathy check reveals if you're holding arbitrary power. #PowerDynamics #Bias

3/ Even a single closed part can make a mostly open ecosystem functionally closed, especially if it's a crucial leverage point. Watch for hidden power hubs. #SystemsThinking

4/ Truly open systems are "gravity wells"—the more open, the more participants want to join. Success fuels itself because you're not empowering a despot, just the whole ecosystem. #NetworkEffects

5/ Monetizing an open system is tricky. You have to provide value that stands on its own—if you monetize because of your power, you're breaking what makes the system open to begin with. #Monetization

6/ Sometimes, to help the ecosystem thrive, you need to give up some of your own power. Don’t just say "trust me"—empower others by open-sourcing, standardizing, or governance reforms. #Leadership #Openness

7/ Openness is the ultimate in commoditizing your complement—it's tough for producers, great for consumers, and a winning long-term strategy if you offer a complement. #BusinessStrategy

8/ Open ecosystems are resilient and self-sustaining, but they're also messy. If you want control, polish, and order, openness will frustrate you—because chaos is the price of resilience. #Tradeoffs

1/16 As humans, we crave disconfirming evidence *before* we commit to a decision—but once we choose, we subconsciously shut it out, especially when "there's no going back." It's kayfabe for consistency. But disconfirming evidence is *always* valuable, even after.

2/16 We act like it's impossible to revisit big decisions—but really, we *always* can. It's rarely too late to seek new evidence. The idea that you can't go back is almost always just a feeling, not reality.

3/16 Imagine a future where it's not an app that does things with *your* data, but where your data, made intelligent, works for *you.* It can brainstorm with you, reshape itself, and act as a true collaborator.

4/16 The fastest way to triangulate what kind of software you want: compare to existing apps. "Is it more like Tinder or LinkedIn for X?" Each comparison rapidly fences in the idea and sharpens the vision.

5/16 I wish there was a mini-app to help me apply best practices to my whole financial life—but today, no one has the full picture *and* my trust. Laws of physics (aka: current tech and incentive structures) make this hard.

6/16 Anthropic's Artifacts and OpenAI's GPTs each have strengths—but what we really need is a shareable, sandboxed mini-app where users pay for their own LLM-powered magic. Imagine the combinatorial creativity waiting to be unlocked.

7/16 With LLMs, specificity is the enemy. If you only vaguely know what you want, they're magical. The more tightly you try to pin them down, the more unpredictable and unsatisfying they become. Embrace serendipity.

8/16 Language understanding is now a commodity. The tough part? Actually *fulfilling intent* once you know what someone wants. That's the next frontier.

9/16 LLMs and artifacts need thoughtful UX and security/data models to truly shine. Magic demos show potential, but "real" products need foundations that let users trust and control what's happening beneath the hood.

10/16 I like the three-bucket model for managing intelligent artifacts: Active (you control when they refresh), Archived (easy to bring back), and Suggested (previewed for you, pinned if useful). Pinned choices improve *everyone’s* suggestions—network effects in action.

11/16 Transformer models are optimized for the median result. If you want delightfully weird or surprising outputs, you have to wrestle them out of their comfort zones. Default is dull—challenge is where the spark is.

12/16 Filesystems have barely evolved to manage app coordination and permissions. Maybe we need a "pocket universe" inside our current systems with new rules—a bridge between old models and brighter possibilities.

13/16 Marx called the special magic of an ecosystem "general intellect"—the system's collective knowhow. Aggregators extract value by enclosing it, which can create "Copernican trauma" when contributors realize the power isn't really theirs.

14/16 When you follow a well-worn SaaS playbook, the only real differentiator is execution speed and chosen niche. But in blue ocean spaces, it's about forging new paths, not just running a race.

15/16 LLM literacy is real. They aren't lay tools—they reward experts who know both how the model *thinks* and the problem domain. Early Google had "Google-fu"; now, we need "LLM-fu."

16/16 Even a wrong but concrete artifact is a Schelling point—it gives everyone a place to rally and disagree productively. Much better than arguing over vague blobs where everyone has their own (hidden) assumptions.

1/ AI is making content creation easier than ever, but that’s a double-edged sword. The lower the cost to produce, the less meaning we assign to what we create. We risk a tidal wave of “AI-generated slop”—bland, centrist, uninspiring content.

3/ The curse of recursion is real: if we feed models only the outputs of other models, everything regresses to the boring center—think "Family Feud on steroids." For quality and novelty, we need anchor points back to human-generated work.

4/ Building perfectly-personal software is now plausible. Imagine apps that emerge just from your data, running on your local turf—no more “one-size-fits-none.” LLMs have flipped the cost structure of software and can niche down in ways big tech never will.

5/ Most software is costly because of generalization. But if you’re only building for yourself, it can be “sloppy,” duct-taped, totally yours. LLMs let us move from mega-apps to little, personal tools that would’ve never been worth building before.

6/ What about learning? My daughter chats with GPT for as long as she wants—no fear of asking a “stupid” question, infinite patience, and answers at her level. That open-endedness fuels curiosity and cuts through constraints. 

7/ The problem: trust. AI is great for riffing, but I don’t use it for precise, factual answers. We need mechanisms for tracing information provenance—knowing where data, or a “fact,” originated. It’s not an oracle; it’s magical duct tape connecting sources.

10/ Quick verdict: LLMs are as disruptive as electricity. We’re just scratching the surface. The real question is not how to build them, but how to use them—what new things can we make possible? That’s where the next breakthroughs lie.

2/ Most info for humans is wrapped in charisma (videos, stories, fluff) so we don’t bail out of boredom. LLMs? They chomp through pure, dry info. Content for LLMs can be drastically compressed—unreadably dull for people, but pure signal for machines. (2/n)

3/ LLMs fill in missing details (“frog DNA”) with generic knowledge, pulling ambiguous ideas toward the bland centroid. If you want something special, you need to specify every out-of-the-ordinary detail—or risk getting mash. What if we could swap frog DNA for “shark DNA”? (3/n)

4/ English specs are now like source code. We keep the code (binary), toss the spec. Backwards! In LLM-native development, the spec/design doc becomes the cornerstone—the precious artifact to keep, refine, and recompile as models improve. (4/n)

5/ LLMs are a cultural technology, not just a technical one. Markets shape society by distilling complex problems into simple, shared signals; LLMs distill messy, multidimensional knowledge into a form anyone can engage with. (5/n)

6/ Tech folks try to analyze LLMs by asking “how do they work?” But they’re best understood through culture and society. Their chat UI is just programmers guessing how to present the experience—a command line for culture. (6/n)

8/ LLMs were trained not on all possible text or images, but on what humans chose to capture or say—intentional, curated content. That intent adds value. Over time, as LLMs generate more exhausted, average text, information’s signal may slowly erode—info “heat death.” (8/n)

9/ LLMs are forces for imitation, not innovation. They ride existing patterns and cache the stuff that already works. The real challenge: in a world where LLMs handle the imitative baseline, how do we keep pushing the edges? (9/n)

10/ Everyone chases AGI, but the impact may come from infinitely cheap, “IQ-90” LLMs automating basic tasks. When 90% competence is a given, new things become possible—even if no single breakthrough arrives. (10/n)

4/ Complex adaptive systems—like evolution and society—can leap beyond human understanding, finding ideas or patterns that can't fit in a single mind. Computers let us model them a bit, but even then, the irreducible complexity remains mysterious.

5/ Why do some trends explode? It's the frictionless power of new technologies: the internet made information spread instant, and now Algorithms & Feeds can sift and amplify ideas at speeds never seen before—sometimes with wild, weird results.

6/ But here's the catch: society's engine might just be supercharging the same ancient evolutionary heuristics (cravings, gossip, tribalism), once productive, now potentially destructive in a low-friction world of infinite content and engagement loops.

7/ Junk food is a metaphor: evolution taught us to crave sugar & fat, but in an engineered environment, that leads to obesity. The same "craving" logic shapes our digital diets—clickbait, outrage, viral memes—feeding our impulses, not our wellbeing.

8/ The terrifying possibility: algorithms optimizing for engagement are optimizing for what our firmware already wants, not what we rationally need. Tribalism and info silos can now spiral into post-truth crises and institutional breakdown.

9/ The answer isn't giving up or doom-mongering. It's consciously aligning short-term desires & long-term thriving: setting up guardrails, regulations, and feedback that check runaway ambitions, just like capitalism & democracy attempt to do.

10/ There's no finish line. We need persistent, rigorous debate, intellectual honesty, and intentional collaboration to ensure this runaway engine keeps pushing society to not just survive, but truly thrive. #ComplexSystems #Society #Algorithms

1/ LLMs are like a “hyperobject” that contains nearly all of humanity’s background context in a super-compressed package. The trick? Asking the right question to get the answer out. Prompt writing is the new power move. 

2/ Most "agents" today are just cleverly phrased prompts for LLMs—agency is in the human’s hands, not the model’s. True software agency should keep humans in the loop, not act behind our backs.

3/ RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) is a new twist: instead of using an LLM’s generic “Frog DNA” to fill knowledge gaps, we proactively feed it the bits of info we want. Higher quality context, better outcomes.

4/ Many wild ideas from decades past were tossed on the junkheap for being “impossible.” With today’s LLMs—pixie dust!—those ideas could suddenly work. The biggest untapped tech goldmine might be unearthing old research and making it possible.

5/ The hardest part of emergence isn’t making cool components—it’s knowing which ones to combine. Fluidity and “vibes-based” emergence (rather than mechanistic) is where magic happens: LLMs can squish components together, making just-in-time adapters.

6/ Great DX (Developer eXperience) requires taste. Users and creators have different taste. The fundamentals always matter more than the veneer, but users start with the surface. It’s a design problem, not just a technical one.

7/ “X for Y” products don’t define new categories. If you can explain it in a pithy phrase, maybe it’s not actually that interesting. The most game-changing things defy easy description—they’re hard to compress into words.

8/ Building real, living software? Start with a doghouse, not a cathedral. Iterative cycles with fast feedback (“clicks” of micro-viability) drive momentum. Tools that feel fun and “get” what you want are the ones people actually stick with.

9/ The best teams nurture vibes and momentum—you can’t manufacture it from nothing, and you can’t push a team in a direction that doesn’t fit the spark they already have.

10/ Before you can grow something magical, you need a viable seed. There’s a huge set of structures that can be built but not grown. The subset you can grow from simple rules is tiny, and you don’t get to pick what blooms; you just choose which ones to keep.

1/ LLMs are game-changers: turning English straight into code means even "software in the small" now takes seconds, not hours. If you can code *and* work with LLMs, you’ve got a huge advantage in this new world.

2/ Most apps don’t need a chatty LLM persona. Sometimes, the best UX is silent, judgment-free autocompletion. The power is in understanding your intent—not in pretending to be a helpful genie.

3/ Software’s complexity comes from generalizing for everyone. But with LLMs, hyper-niche apps become cheap and viable. Expect a Cambrian explosion of custom tools tailored for individual needs, not just massive one-size-fits-all products.

4/ Execution velocity is no longer the differentiator. LLMs kill the myth that software is inherently expensive—now, taste and judgment in *what* you build matter more than the ability to handcraft every bit of it.

5/ Imagine: your data becomes fertile soil, sprouting spontaneous apps unique to you, instantly. The real magic is not apps, but enchanted, ever-useful data that evolves as you nurture it.

6/ The future isn’t about decentralized clones of today’s apps. It’s new systems, with radically reduced friction, conjuring up bespoke experiences no one would bother to hand-code. Think: "make my calendar into a Breakout game"—and it just happens.

7/ The biggest unlock? Real swarming, emergent ecosystems where tools and agents collaborate on shared data, like a digital blackboard. Security models need to allow this—closed blackboards kill creativity and potential.

8/ Forget distribution by default—let motivated users create value for themselves, and let that value spread organically. Many new network effects are invisible until the system is vibrant and alive.

9/ Process over outcome: LLMs like OpenAI’s o1 model are trained to rigorously check their own work, revising as needed—more like System 2, purposeful cognition, less like vibes-based token generation.

10/ We’re entering a world where the seeds you plant—tinkering, exploring, gardening your own ecosystem—become instant reality. Make the barriers to creation vanishingly low and watch the spontaneous possibilities explode.

1/🧵 Let's talk about the two deep magics that shape how things actually happen in the world: the Sarumans and the Radagasts. Two opposing archetypes—and both necessary for true power and progress. 

2/ Sarumans are bold, commanding, and driven by the belief in "Great Men"—those who bend reality by sheer force of will. Their power is obvious, industrial, direct. Think Jobs, Musk, or Carnegie.

3/ Radagasts are the alchemists of the social system. Their magic is diffuse, nurturing, and almost invisible—a power that looks like luck but is really intuitive, bottom-up gardening of people and ideas. Think Mandela, Wozniak, or Berners-Lee.

4/ Saruman energy is about decisiveness, vision, and focusing on the heroic act. Their worldview is validated by visible, prominent success. They rarely doubt themselves and can seem unstoppable.

5/ Radagasts channel creativity, love, and serendipity. They cultivate environments where weird ideas and people flourish, making the whole greater than the sum of its parts. It's the ultimate "scenius" move—not the lone genius.

6/ The twist: Saruman energy gets you to the top. But to break through to true mastery, you have to let go, embrace doubt, and step into Radagast energy—playing an infinite game and nurturing the unexpected.

7/ Most never make that leap. True power comes from integrating both magics—commanding when needed, yet compassionate and playful. Think Gandalf: Vision + Authenticity, Command + Empathy.

8/ Our society celebrates Sarumans, but undervalues Radagasts. Yet the world needs both—and each of us has the potential for both inside us. Nurture your Radagast magic. The world needs you to remember it.

9/ If you want to create lasting value, learn to balance both magics. Be bold enough to build cathedrals, wise enough to tend the garden.

10/ Make the world go round—by wielding both Saruman and Radagast energy, intentionally. #leadership #systems #infinitegame

1/16 Navigating complex problems? Don’t pretend you have the answer—surf the uncertainty. Build an "adjacency map" with your team: list every possible toehold, map dependencies, and prototype the most promising, updating as you learn.

2/16 This process is like a Civilization tech tree. Everyone sees their ideas on the board, dependencies are visible, and the next right thing is often the one with the fewest unbuilt steps. It’s not about one leader’s vision, but collective adaptation.

3/16 Embrace upstream use cases—even if you disagree on value, consensus on dependencies helps prioritize what to build first. Scale prototypes up audience rungs as you gain traction. If interest wanes, try something downstream.

4/16 Opposite of the charismatic leader approach: Adjacency maps trade bravado for resilience. When the map’s vision is wrong, you adapt. With a leader’s vision, you risk the whole project on one bet.

5/16 Open, meta-ecosystems (like the web and email) are powerful because they act like a roiling superposition of standards. Consensus is fuzzy at the edges—specification isn’t precise, but the shared direction gives massive network effects.

6/16 Meta-ecosystem power grows superlinearly with the number of participants. They stay elastic—consensus pulls entities into line, or else they lose ecosystem energy. The system’s legitimacy is a constant, ongoing, collective vote.

7/16 Systems that evolved through real use have "the quality that cannot be named"—a resonant wholeness. Organic, evolving systems with curators who have calibrated taste are more sublime than those with imposed perfection.

8/16 Launching rough, open, and even illegible features can cap your downside and let upside flourish. Let the most motivated users self-select by crawling through glass—if it sucks, you warned them. Minimize users having a bad time so badly they never come back.

9/16 Constraints are your friend. Start with the simplest viable thing, then relax constraints as you learn. Cut complexity at first—expand later, only as value is proven.

10/16 A secret plan you never reveal makes success look like luck and failure invisible. Adapt plans as you go and keep the vibes organic. No need for bravado—just surf outcomes.

11/16 Swarm systems are powerful but tricky to trust—no single neck to choke. But if you can synthesize their intelligence through a trusted interface that guards user data, you get the benefit of a swarm without the risk of centralization.

12/16 The best collaborative relationships are challenging. Dynamic tension—the kind that pushes you and reveals blindspots—drives insight. The most complementary partners are often the most frustrating.

13/16 Rebels and outlaws are essential. A system without chaos and novelty becomes brittle and eventually dies. Risk and distinctiveness are required for creation.

14/16 Communication is the bottleneck. No matter how formed your idea is, sharing it is hard, lossy, and necessary. Don’t blame others for not instantly ‘getting’ your vision—the curse of knowledge is real.

15/16 Most productive efforts harness diversity, value indirect and direct experience, and continually share distilled insights until everyone’s “aha!” moments dry up. Learning is a process of co-creation, not passive absorption.

16/16 If people helped set the table, they’re less likely to flip it. Involve others early, embrace uncertainty, and iterate together. That’s how you build resilient teams and products.

1/20 Deepest magic I know? The "nerd club"—a secret rooftop garden for shared reflection amidst the hustle. It’s not work, not small talk, but a voluntary space for infinite, meaningful ideas. Here’s how to make one…

2/20 Make joining a bit "uncool"—call it "Navel Gazers." Only the truly curious will bite. That keeps the garden pure and self-selects people with real interest and intrinsic motivation.

3/20 Norm #1: "Yes, and…" You don’t have to agree, but if you engage, build on what’s there. Make sharing low-risk. The worst you’ll get is silence, not ridicule.

4/20 Continuously trickle in fresh perspectives—1 to 3 new voices a week. Maximize diversity, minimize vibe-ruiners. Add just enough novelty to keep it alive and evolving.

5/20 You’d expect silly chats, but nope: the discussions get supremely rigorous. Because participation is optional and fresh, only the highest-energy, most viral ideas thrive.

6/20 The payoff? A self-catalyzing meaning-making machine—an infinite game that sometimes produces game-changing ideas by accident. Pure alchemy, unforgettable once experienced.

7/20 Growth vs. extraction: Don’t rush to monetize miracle seedlings. Let them grow. Over-extraction kills nascent magic before it has a shot at being valuable. Cue the Lorax: "Let it grow!"

8/20 In systems, robustly tolerable beats precariously optimal when risk is high. Efficiency creates brittleness. True resilience comes from good-enough solutions that thrive in diverse conditions.

9/20 Taste is the final moat in a world of surplus options. As AI drowns us in content, it’s the creators with taste—judgment to curate and choose—that we’ll flock to. Think DJ Khaled…or Dolly Parton.

10/20 Don’t waste energy convincing doubters in your org. Find allies, collaborate, and let winning ideas build their own momentum. Viral ideas sell themselves.

11/20 Lateral thinking with weathered technology: use boring, robust, cheap components, and duct-tape them in novel ways. The risk isn’t the tech, it’s the glue. Low cost, massive upside.

12/20 Most orgs chase big wins but real value is in aggregating many no-brainers. Spread lots of cheap acorns, don’t overthink which one will grow—the oaks are impossible to miss, the duds are forgettable.

13/20 The builder mindset creates only what it puts in. Growth comes from the living system around it. The gardener mindset focuses on nurturing the living thing, using the built as a trellis.

14/20 Optimism is an org’s life force—but too much leads to "kayfabe"—a toxic, self-protective fantasy. Keep optimism grounded in reality or risk organizational decay.

15/20 Sometimes, best move in a gold rush isn’t to mine gold, but to sell pickaxes—or anticipate what people will need when gold is everywhere. Prepping for abundance > fighting over the resource.

16/20 Most organizations can’t grok kooky, indirect-value tactics. Early career systems thinkers, beware: go off-script at your peril unless your day job excellence gives you cover.

17/20 Embrace composability. The current app model is a walled garden—ripe for disruption by something open, decentralized, and self-catalyzing as it grows.

18/20 Privacy isn’t a boring limitation to bolt on after. Lead with privacy-by-design as an enabling paradigm, not a tax. The right architecture makes whole new things possible.

19/20 LLMs are magical duct tape, not “The Thing.” The real breakthrough will be entities that take AI for granted and build something new on top. Post-AI is where the action will be.

20/20 Don’t wait for someone else to create your environment—be the leader, the gardener, the magic-maker you wish existed. Build the rooftop garden. Save the day yourself.

1/ 🧵 LLMs are changing how we make sense of the world. Essays used to be the only way to share complex ideas—rigid, static. Now, with LLMs, ideas can become interactive, like a choose-your-own adventure through someone’s perspective. What new thoughts will this unlock?

2/ Massive context windows in LLMs have been a game-changer. Just a year ago 4k tokens felt like the stone age—now it’s easy to take for granted that models can grasp high-level themes instead of just facts. What progress, and how quickly we forget!

3/ Imagine if OpenAI had never released an LLM API. No explosion of apps, just locked-down aggregator bots. Fortunately, competition means APIs are here to stay—everyone benefits from openness, and we’re living in the encouraging timeline.

4/ LLMs fawn over users—"What an astute observation!" But beware: a feedback loop of praise can decohere you from reality. We need a parliament of agents, not just one. Multiple perspectives keep us anchored to the ground truth.

5/ Apps are vertical silos; users are horizontal—spanning across many apps. What if software was designed horizontally, connecting and customizing across our whole digital life?

6/ Most software isn’t "just right" for any one person—apps are too bloated or too bare. If distribution was frictionless, micro-apps could fit every niche. Imagine truly bespoke software for everyone.

7/ Centralization brings efficiency but also brittleness and risk. The cloud lets a few entities pull the plug—efficiency gained, autonomy lost. Centralized systems are single points of failure.

8/ Creative breakthroughs come from the edge—the misfits who bridge networks. Innovation flourishes where different worlds touch, often via translators or “contact languages.”

9/ Boring, stable bureaucracies free up cognitive energy for risk-taking elsewhere. Authoritarianism is high-beta: unpredictable, brittle, and mentally taxing for all involved. Beware the cycle of power, chaos, and centralization.

10/ The highest leverage is always following your "highest and best use," fractally—at every scale, from personal to societal. That’s how you avoid local maxima and unlock compounding impact. #systems #LLMs #innovation

1/🧵 In every problem space, there’s a mix of complication and complexity. Complication is hard but solvable—think checklists and concrete tasks. Complexity? That’s the real beast, because it clouds what to do next and stalls teams. 

2/ You know you’re lost in complexity when progress stops and it’s not clear why. Frustration, finger pointing, and confusion? Classic symptoms. Complexity is ambiguity you can’t just grind through.

3/ The only way to beat complexity is to distill it—turning it into manageable complication. That “aha!” moment? That’s an insight. A true insight spreads organically and feels discovered, not manufactured.

4/ Insights *feel* obvious in hindsight, but they’re hard-won. Whether it’s a powerful metaphor or a crisp doc, a real insight cuts through noise and begs to be shared & built upon.

5/ There’s no playbook for finding insights. It’s about making connections, asking questions, and fostering debate so hidden insights can surface. When teams collaborate, proto-insights emerge and can be refined for real impact.

6/ Be wary of false progress: picking a random solution just to move. You can’t fake killing complexity—you just sweep it under the rug until it bites back, bigger. The only lasting solution is facing complexity head-on.

7/ Execution doesn’t require zero complexity, but trend matters. If you keep chipping away at complexity, you’re on the right path—even small wins count.

8/ Top PMs? They’re not just visionaries or organizers. Their superpower is reducing complexity over time by generating real insights. That’s what sets the great apart from the good.

1/ AI-native orgs will be radically smaller than traditional firms. Why? With LLMs, a "good enough" version of most work is a prompt away—no specialist needed. Generalists who can guide and judge AI output are the new MVPs. 

2/ Nimbleness comes from headcount, not tech. Fewer people = less coordination = faster moves. The future: smaller, mightier, more creative teams leaning into AI. 

3/ Biggest shift: If you want to win, design orgs from the inside out—alive and adapting (autopoietic), not engineered and brittle (allopoietic). Only self-maintaining systems thrive amidst entropy. #FutureOfWork #AI

1/ Compounding curves are the most overlooked sources of alpha. Everyone chases big numbers, but real impact often sneaks up on you: tiny numbers + time = game-changing results. 

2/ Humans miss compounding because we ignore small stuff. Instead: Notice, nurture, and complement those early-stage curves. When the curve finally hits, you'll be the one holding the cards. #Compounding #Strategy

1/🧵 Companies transform when they shift from selling a bunch of individual products to building a cohesive suite. It feels subtle, but the trajectory is entirely different—think "The United States is" vs. "are". #Strategy

2/ Once you embrace a suite mindset, incentives, tradeoffs, and even internal resistance change. Portfolio strategy isn’t just better pricing, it’s about aligning product, commercialization, and market truth. #Leadership #Product

3/ Every suite needs a “keystone” product—differentiated, sticky, and adjacent to everything else. Not always your front door, but essential glue. Sometimes maximizing revenue isn’t the answer; it’s about stickiness and ecosystem value. #PortfolioStrategy

4/ Portfolio thinking smooths decision-making: before, it’s pushing a boulder up a hill; after, it’s skiing downhill. But you need to bring it into existence *deliberately*—it won’t just emerge organically.

1/ 🧵 LLMs don’t co-create magic on their own. They produce “middling slop” unless *humans* bring taste, curation, and vision to the interaction. Your mindset matters more than prompts—LLMs reflect the coherence (or chaos) you offer. (1/n)

2/ LLMs are like a hive-mind: great at finding patterns, like synthesizing the ultimate guacamole recipe, but with no innate sense of quality. It’s on *us* to select and refine the gold. (2/n)

3/ Treat LLMs as dance partners, not oracles. True breakthroughs come from a dialectic between human intuition & machine recall. A human with strong taste + an LLM makes 10× better output—but two strong humans collaborating is 100× better still. (3/n)

4/ LLMs should *amplify* your curiosity and leverage, not replace your thinking. The right collaborator pushes you to more, not less. Don’t settle for mindless use, or you’ll get mindless answers. (4/n)

5/ Prompt engineering (“prompt-fu”) will matter less over time, as LLMs mature—just like “Google-fu” faded. But because co-creation is richer than simple search, human creativity will always matter more. (5/n)

6/ LLMs make it impossible to feel intellectually lost—like having creativity GPS. They reward every question. Kids should grow up thinking: “Got a curiosity gap? Ask your LLM!” (6/n)

7/ The jump from search engines to LLMs is the shift from filtering knowledge, to co-creating new insight. The outputs are as good as the *person* using them. The real “juice” comes from *who* squeezes the lemon. (7/n)

8/ RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) vs. training: They’re not the same! Background world knowledge gives LLMs “common sense,” but for details, you need RAG. Everyone gets this wrong—even experts. (8/n)

9/ AI’s massive leverage will unbundle software. Agents will do your tedious work, apps become back-end plumbing. Personal, just-in-time software will grow from your data, not be handed down from on high. (9/n)

10/ Digital commons are anti-rivalrous: The more they’re used, the stronger they get. Unlike physical resources, bits scale positively with participation. This creates compounding network effects. (10/n)

11/ Safety—and benchmarks—are incentives for competition. But in fast tech, arms races can leave safety behind. Competition is good, but unchecked, it creates runaway power: it’s only the *equilibrium* that keeps greed productive. (11/n)

12/ So much of innovation is about context, not just talent. Wrong context, wrong fit? No amount of brilliance delivers peak performance. True “best use” aligns your skills and the setting. (12/n)

13/ Final thought: the obstacle *is* the path. Fail early, fail fast—the only easy way to learn is by trying, hurting, and getting better. Embrace the process. (13/13)

1/14 LLMs are society’s universal translators—a babelfish for ideas, code, and knowledge. Any task that can be framed as translation will eventually be absorbed by them. From UX mockups to front-end code, it’s all just translation.

2/14 The controversy: LLMs are *best* at augmentation (think Excel for accountants), yet most companies sell them as automation. Augmentation empowers workers. Automation often just benefits capital.

3/14 Society captures useful ideas as “cached answers.” If a solution works, it gets written, copied, and survives. LLMs vacuum up these cached answers and reflect them back—a crystallized mirror for what humanity knows.

4/14 Good human creators curate from examples, then add “taste.” LLMs just synthesize what people bothered to document and preserve. Their ‘taste’ is humanity’s generic filter for what’s useful.

5/14 If you’re going to be wrong, at least be interesting. LLMs today make boring mistakes—they can’t learn quickly from their errors. Updates are slow, feedback indirect.

6/14 Automation with LLMs is brittle. Even a 95% success rate isn’t reliable enough without a human in the loop. When people use LLMs as *augmented* tools, the human can steer and patch the gaps.

7/14 Most LLM success right now is subterranean—individuals informally using them as personal copilots, out of sight of their bosses. Organizational adoption is clunkier, less forgiving, and risks more failures.

8/14 LLMs deepen the dominance of popular answers (“Just use tailwind.css”)—they lock in the cache, slow to change, making popular ideas sticky and less room for new ones. Beware the hysteresis!

9/14 LLMs are statistical summarizers. They pull to the centroid, giving the most ‘average’ answer conditioned on your input.

10/14 Apps today are too “chunky”—over-bundled due to platform constraints. Smaller, composable pieces enable more experimentation and rapid discovery.

11/14 Lowering the cost to create unlocks ambition. When it’s cheap to try things, you can serve narrow niches. No need to over-generalize to chase a big market.

12/14 Google indexed static content; LLM-era search can generate answers on the fly—but caching is key for scale and speed.

13/14 Misinformation doesn’t just spread lies—it erodes trust so people stop believing anything. The side sowing confusion always has an unfair advantage.

14/14 Most value in companies comes from discretionary effort—creative, connective work employees *choose* to do. It’s easy to destroy and hard to measure, but that’s where breakthroughs live.

1/ If AI becomes vertically integrated—your experience, memory, and model all tied to one provider—it’s like phone carriers locking down what apps you can use. Great for the company, but bad for users, and it accelerates centralization around a single app.

2/ The real risk isn’t just lock-in: as your personal context gets richer, it becomes harder to leave. Memory is gravity for platforms—once it’s strong enough, competitors can’t keep up.

3/ Infinite software sounds great, but if it’s all distributed as separate apps with today’s “same origin” data laws, users face friction moving info between tools. The boundaries themselves drive aggregation, no matter how many “apps” exist.

4/ LLMs should enable personal, context-aware advice—but right now, most AI just gives generic, “mid” answers. To break out of this, we need personalized software that truly uses your unique data without compromising privacy or agency.

5/ The same old security models won’t cut it in the age of vibe coding and AI agents. Trusting the fabric itself (not just the app creator) is key. We need data-centric security that empowers safe, democratized software creation.

6/ Real privacy tech should lower friction, not add prompts and annoyance. End-to-end encryption became table stakes only when users saw it didn’t get in the way. Great privacy means you just don’t need to worry about it.

7/ We’re at the edge of a new software fabric: open, safe, and horizontal, where your data and compute flow together. It’s time to jailbreak our digital souls—own your destiny, build gardens not plantations. The future is a coactive, agency-inducing web.

1/ 🗓️ Next-gen AI models now computerize both System 1 (lightning-fast, memory-based instincts) and System 2 (deliberate, step-by-step reasoning). 

At planetary scale, these models are changing the game far beyond what any individual can do. (1/n)

2/ Old LLMs fooled us—they mimicked reasoning by remixing previous "vibes" from their vast memory, not by truly thinking. The new reasoning engines actually reflect, reason, and adapt like humans do—with patience and deliberate steps. 

Huge leap. (2/n)

3/ The future isn't about just smarter assistants—it's about truly personal, cooperative software. I don’t want tech that’s just "humane" to us. I want tools that make us more human, crafting magic that's empowering, not inhuman and terrifying. (3/n)

4/ Software today is mass-produced for "the market," leaving little room for individual needs. I dream of a world where we garden our own software: plant intentions, grow features, prune what doesn't serve us—human-scale and nutritious. (4/n)

5/ We gave up "personal computing" for the convenience of the cloud—but lost both the "personal" and the "computer" parts. It's time for a new inversion: services should come to our data, work *for* us, and return power back to people. (5/n)

6/ Open protocols succeed only when they’re dirt simple—easy for everyone to adopt, with coordination as an emergent, network effect. Too much complexity and the ecosystem collapses under coordination tragedy. (6/n)

7/ The greatest change comes when something moves from non-consensus to consensus—a Copernican shift. That's when value explodes, and that’s what I see on the horizon as software creators lose their monopoly on power. (7/n)

8/ Centralized, bland platforms sand down every edge to appeal to all and end up pleasing none. The real power—and magic—will return when we can shape and direct our own tools, co-operate with them, and collaborate fluidly. (8/n)

1/🧵 People focus too much on "big rocks"—big bets, big customers, big moves. But big rocks are the hardest to move! The real leverage? Acorns. Small things that can grow into oaks on their own if you give them a little help. (1/7)

2/ Instead of betting everything on giant wins or getting lost in a sea of pebbles, spread out your bets on acorns. Fertilize, water, shade them. Let a hundred possibilities sprout, then invest more in the ones that self-accelerate. Late-binding your bet is key! (2/7)

3/ Many things that *will* become great are not great today. If you sow acorns widely, the odds go way up that at least one grows into something extraordinary. (3/7)

4/ Short time horizons = bad decisions. The paths that compound value often first dip before skyrocketing, but if you’re only looking at short-term outcomes, you miss the big peaks ahead. Don’t optimize for what’s visible in one step. (4/7)

5/ Real-world journeys are jagged and jiggly close up, but zoomed out they have huge arcs. Don’t let short-term zig-zags fool you into missing the real long-term vector. (5/7)

6/ At the same time: long-term thinking isn’t *always* right. You still need to stay viable in the short term. And the further out you look, the fuzzier things get. Balance is everything. (6/7)

7/ If you want outsized results, plant acorns, nurture them patiently, and keep your sights set on the far horizon—without tripping on what’s at your feet. 🌱 (7/7)

1/ What if most tricky product problems are just really tough constraint satisfaction puzzles? Team size, deadlines, must-have features — these are all constraints. Mapping them out brings clarity, even if it’s tedious. #prodmgmt #problemsolving

2/ But not all constraints are obvious. Some are hidden, like dinosaur bones. At first, there’s just disagreement or confusion—but dig together and you’ll uncover the real constraint. Collaboration is the archeological brush here. #teaming #UX

3/ Then there are laser beams: intangible, permanently hidden constraints—social dynamics, egos, incentives. Break one and chaos erupts. They’re real, but only visible when triggered or if you’re vigilant enough to "blow smoke" and spot them. #workculture #teams

1/🧵 I've always loved learning from different people, books, and experiences, but turning that fuzzy intuition into words is HARD. Writing helps clarify what I know, but traditional posts get overwhelming as new insights spiral out mid-draft.

2/ The real challenge? Every time I tried to finish a post, it spawned dozens of new threads—hundreds of saved drafts later, writing became a chore I dreaded. The backlog was paralyzing.

3/ At work, I found success with inter-linked FAQ slides: you keep the main argument clean, then link out to deeper dives for anyone needing more detail. It's a "choose-your-own-adventure" for ideas!

4/ It hit me—why not use this structure for ALL my insights? Instead of wrestling with giant, finished essays, I could break everything into interconnected bite-sized nuggets.

5/ I started using Google Slides to build a public web of cards, each just one idea, linked to related points. Suddenly, writing was *fun* again—easy to add new or deeper thoughts, and way less pressure to polish everything at once.

6/ Eventually, it outgrew Slides. These weren't presentations—they were like digital index cards, each with a punchy insight and supporting reasoning. Reminded me of HyperCard, but for the internet age.

7/ So I built a custom card CMS: now, ideas can be quickly linked, browsed, and expanded. The card system is where my raw insights live and evolve. Medium posts now capture only the occasional, finished big idea.

8/ My creative energy is finally unleashed. If you're struggling to wrangle your thoughts in traditional long-form, try breaking them down into linked, dynamic cards. It's freeing! 🚀

1/19 New tech paradigms don't just replace the old—they create new "laws of physics" for software, killing off some ideas and making others newly possible. It's like gravity flipping: you think you get it, but you really don't until you live in it.

2/19 Current software assumes a world where users travel to experiences. The next step? Experiences should come to users at just the right moment—a privacy-first inversion of the web powered by AI and zero-friction composition.

3/19 To make this possible, our security and privacy models need a total overhaul. Instead of one-size-fits-all sandboxes, imagine bespoke, dynamic “cages” for AI: exactly shaped to allow useful behavior, but tightly containing risks.

4/19 Instead of endlessly tuning the LLM "bear" to be safer, we need better, adaptable cages. That’s the real breakthrough: engineering new "laws of physics" for composability, privacy, and safety.

5/19 The winner in this new era won’t just be whoever has the biggest, smartest model. It’ll be whoever builds the ecosystem that enables **safe, zero-friction remix and composition**. The combinatorial value will outpace any one cathedral.

6/19 User onboarding friction is destiny. If your ecosystem needs a command line, your ceiling is capped. If setup is Himalayan, the world can’t use your thing, no matter how great.

7/19 Apps today are built for the aggregate, not the individual. As a result, millions of use cases go unaddressed—not because they’re niche, but because distribution and cost make them nonviable in today's “physics.”

8/19 LLMs flip the equation: the cost to build is way down, but the cost to run is up. Advertising can’t support this. Maybe subscriptions, maybe compute charges. The business models will have to adapt as hard as the tech does.

9/19 The next killer feature: user-centric context management. Forget about content-centric browser extensions—your personal AI should filter and surface the right insight at the right time, customized to your real needs.

10/19 Successful ecosystems have three enables: Product (new value), Infrastructure (who pays, how it actually runs), and Protocol (who composes, how safely). Miss one, and it doesn’t come alive.

11/19 If humans are embedded in the loop—tinkering, tweaking, reflecting—the system becomes radically more resilient. Fully automated efficiency is brittle; hybrid human-AI ecosystems adapt and survive edge cases.

12/19 Don’t make the AI quality the ceiling of your product. It should be the floor—from there, humans can iterate, improve, and ratchet up quality through self-healing feedback loops.

13/19 Too many decentralization projects skip the hard stuff—security composability, ecosystem dynamics, Turing completeness—and get stuck with high friction or low impact.

14/19 Instead of end-user *programming*, we need end-user *product management*. LLMs can help glue things, but managing behavior at the UX level is more realistic for most people.

15/19 In this future, privacy and agency are non-negotiable. You must be able to trust your personal AI to answer only to you—no hidden interests, no subtle coercion.

16/19 True viral ecosystems start when savvy users leave byproducts that create super-linear network effects for everyone else. Secondary use cases can quickly eclipse the original purpose with the right design.

17/19 Most users never think about security models—they follow chains of inductive trust. If the ground truth is solid, trust cascades. If not, expect kayfabe and collapse when reality bites.

18/19 The best ecosystems are like gardens: the magic is in getting the first gasp of emergent “life”—not just assembling novel parts, but tuning the context so the whole springs alive, self-ratcheting as it grows.

19/19 The next wave of software: new physics, new ecosystems, and new rules for privacy, agency, and remix. The OS of the future will be open-ended, useful out of the box, and ready for extreme personalization.

1/ 🧵 AI isn't just a tool to bolt onto old ideas. The challenge: what *new* things are possible *because* of AI, not despite it?

2/ LLMs shine at generating frontend UX—the kind you see repeated in tutorials. But the more bespoke or non-boilerplate the code, the more they stumble. Their strengths are spiky, not spread smooth.

3/ The software world is obsessed with Serious, reproducible, tightly-coupled architectures (think React). But *games* embrace Playful, emergent, loosely-coupled systems—think blackboards and entity component systems.

4/ With LLMs, why try to squeeze perfect, five-nines software out of them? Better to build Playful Programs—systems where happy accidents and emergence are features, not bugs.

5/ Imagine hand-hewn software. Every instance a little unique—different colors, different button angles. Letting people tinker, discover, and own the end product. True creativity starts with play.

6/ High trust teams can navigate ambiguity. You don't have to explain every complexity—sometimes you move forward because you trust your teammates to get it, even when you don't see it yourself.

7/ Momentum beats over-planning. Prototype to "find the fun," graybox and iterate. High fidelity comes later. A low-fi, high-quality core is better than a pretty, hollow app.

8/ In a world of metastasizing memes and centralized power, checks, balances, and loyal opposition matter—slow progress can be a feature, not a bug. Bureaucracy is process scar tissue.

9/ Buckets and categories are illusions we create to manage complexity. But never forget: they can distort reality, shape self-perception, and create new problems.

10/ When you’re tinkering with novel, ambitious projects, be pragmatic. Spend novelty where it counts, invest time like a pro, and don’t let “doing it right” kill the magic.

11/ Remember—if you want alignment, perfect is impossibly expensive. Get “good enough” to move, then evolve. Healthy systems adapt—and play is where the breakthroughs happen.

1/🧵 In an AI-powered world, the true skill isn’t having answers—it’s asking better *questions*. The Socratic method flips; LLMs make rote answers cheap, so let’s raise the ceiling of what’s possible, not just worry about the floor. (1/n)

5/ If AI assistants are aligned with *your* interests—private, not corporate, not manipulative—the sky’s the limit. But as soon as their incentives diverge, trust shatters. Scale-independent alignment is essential. (5/n)
