1/🧵 If you want your company to survive this harsh new tech climate, it’s time to ditch the “grow fast at all costs” eucalyptus mindset & embrace the slow, resilient strategy of the live oak. Here’s why resilience wins in tough times… (1/n)

2/ For years, the Silicon Valley playbook was all about explosive growth—run hard, burn cash, dominate fast. But that only worked in an era of easy money & tailwinds. Now, the environment has changed fundamentally. (2/n)

3/ Your gut isn’t calibrated for today’s reality. What used to guarantee success now risks collapse. High-growth strategies are brittle—like eucalyptus, they snap in storms or go up in flames. Resilience is the new, necessary edge. (3/n)

4/ The rule: Survive, then thrive. First, cap downside risk. Only after you’re durable should you focus on maximizing upside. Rushing for heroics or taking wild bets is a recipe for disaster—survival comes first. (4/n)

5/ Keep your “metabolic rate” low. High burn rates lock you into a fragile position & choke your future flexibility. The lower your overhead, the longer your runway, and the more you can focus on real compounding value. (5/n)

6/ Don’t be a hero. Heroics—firefighting & doing everything yourself—look noble but cripple long-term scalability. If your plan depends on heroics, it’s broken. Delegating & systematizing is mandatory for resilient growth. (6/n)

7/ Constantly ship “good enough.” Ship quickly so you can learn from small failures, not catastrophic ones. Perfect is the enemy of survival—get real-world feedback & iterate toward thriving. (7/n)

8/ Trust is the grease for real teamwork. Build high-trust, diverse teams so people feel safe raising risks & bold ideas. Diversity with trust produces rigorous thinking—crucial in a tough environment. (8/n)

9/ Focus on compounding returns, not linear hacks. If you see a compounding loop—like network effects or delighted users—prioritize it over anything with flat returns. Survive via discipline, thrive via compounding. (9/n)

10/ It’s not about holding out for miracles. Plant lots of cheap seeds—innovative experiments, hackathons, weird ideas. You can’t predict which will sprout, but more seeds mean more upside over time. (10/n)

11/ Stop worshipping fast-growth eucalyptus. Resilience means you outlast the storms, adapt, and achieve enduring success. It’s time for us all to grow like live oaks—broad, deep-rooted, & built to last. 🌳 (11/11)

1/ There’s a world of difference between “good” and “great.” Good is viable, but great stands out—an order of magnitude leap that usually demands far more effort… unless you have a unique edge or genuine momentum. You should focus your energy where you have that edge.

2/ There’s a huge mindset gap between treating something as a rental (short-term, transactional) vs. ownership (long-term, invested). Subtle cues like being unable to personalize your workspace nudge you out of ownership and into rental mode—even if you don’t notice it.

3/ Emergence is wild: the whole can be totally different from the sum of the parts. Tiny biases in a system can go unnoticed individually but become powerful collective forces—sometimes giving rise to ‘Simpson’s Paradox’ in group behavior.

4/ Real magic isn’t about breaking the laws of physics, it’s about how social dynamics produce unexpected results. Emergent systems create beautiful, living things without anyone intending it. Learning to engage with emergence means learning to love the mess.

5/ In B2B software, the things that make a product beautiful often clash with what makes it viable in the enterprise. Enterprise needs stickiness and customizability; beautiful abstractions often aren’t customizable—unless customizability itself is the beauty (think Lego Dots).

6/ Building a resilient ecosystem means giving up control so others can adapt and extend your product. The best way: create timeless, flexible abstractions rather than perfect standalone products. True value comes from enabling others to build on top.

7/ Beware Goodhart’s Law: whatever you optimize for, you’ll get in unexpected forms. Incentives morph outcomes in ways you didn’t foresee. No matter how perfect your plan, the emergent system finds loopholes—sometimes burning the furniture just to heat the house.

8/ Startups thrive because their downsides are self-capping—they can tiptoe through the garden like mice. Large enterprises are elephants: they just can’t help but smash things when they try to dance. Let innovation come from the swarm, not the giants.

9/ Book smarts are cheap—practical knowhow is rare and essential. The more certain you are, the more likely you’re blind to what really matters. Creativity and learning demand that you let go of certainties and stay open to the actual messiness of reality.

10/ True illuminators inspire others toward greatness that positively affects whole systems—not just their boss. We all need more of that kind of encouragement, seeded in things with real, emergent potential.

1/ Software shouldn’t be hard blocks—apps. It should be soft, like water: fluid, adaptable, malleable. Users shouldn’t have to change to fit software; software should fit the user. LLMs are closer to this vision than most apps today. 

2/ The tragedy is that we’ve locked software in the box of apps—non-composable monoliths at the mercy of gatekeepers. Software is so much more: it’s alchemy, combinatorial possibility, collective agency. AI just turns up the dial on what’s possible.

3/ Safe, low-friction composability is the real unlock we need. You can’t bolt security on later; it has to be baked in from the start. The web nailed this with link safety and same-origin rules. App frameworks? Not so much.

4/ Don’t decentralize what doesn’t matter. Decentralizing only the database misses the point: the real win is decentralizing the app itself, giving agency and control back to users, not just system architects.

5/ Let’s stop imagining software as little boxed-in products. Let’s build for a world where software flows, composes, and serves human potential—not the other way around. Be water, my friend. #software #future

6/ Writing software is like arcane magic; only wizards could do it. Now, we all can. The shift from control to agency is critical: if the tool extends me, I control it. If it acts with its own agency, I have to trust it—that’s a massive difference.

7/ Most resilient AI-powered experiences will work even without AI. AI should be a bonus, not a single point of failure. Build in ways for users to stay in control, even if the magic glitches.

8/ PMF isn’t about everyone thinking your thing is special—just *enough* people without a vested interest showing you it matters. Most of us are blinded by our own bias here.

9/ Strategy: it’s harder to write a 1-year plan before the 5-year vision. Dream big, settle the direction, then break it down. Specificity early makes you waste a lot of energy retconning the details you want to forget anyway.

10/ Reflect, adapt, and let software be alive. We’re at the start of something wild; don’t think inside today's box. Imagine, iterate, and let the flow happen. #strategy #tech

1/🧵 Large Language Models (LLMs) aren't just tools; they're planetary-scale consensus machines. We study them less like code, more like minds—emergent behavior, fuzzy math, and all. The real action is the social sifting underpinning their training. (1/13)

2/ LLMs are swarm intelligences. The sum of countless tiny human decisions creates emergent outcomes—sometimes magical, sometimes a mess no single user wanted. It's a structured chaos that already rivals early forms of artificial intelligence. (2/13)

3/ Every action we take leaves a "digital residue." LLMs specialize in scooping up this residue and remixing it. They're consensus machines, but retraining them to update this consensus is slow and clunky compared to human swarms. (3/13)

4/ LLMs don't give oracular truth—they offer what "sounds good" based on consensus. This makes them more muse than oracle: they're here to spark, not settle, our thinking. Performative rigor matters. Watch for groupthink in the answers. (4/13)

5/ On coding: point an LLM at software, and it'll YOLO its way to solutions—often causing more messes than value if not managed well. The trick is to act like an engineering manager: demand plans, compare alternatives, veto nonsense. (5/13)

6/ Real innovation isn't in automating what humans already do perfectly. It's in automating things people don't bother with now—creating net new value out of the forgotten, the cozy, the below-the-Coasian-floor use cases. (6/13)

7/ Most new tools struggle to escape the "power user" ghetto. Greasemonkey, MCP, vibe-coding—all assume high trust, command-line comfort, and local use. Mass adoption demands we sand off these edges *without* sacrificing creativity or security. (7/13)

8/ We need a new substrate—like Geocities for vibecoding. Let anyone remix their digital life, open and private, with security baked in. A sandbox that's safe and collaborative makes the creation of micro-apps radically more accessible. (8/13)

9/ Platforms win by reducing friction for what devs *already* crawl through broken glass to do. The next layer of innovation is making micro-tools for personal, non-venture-backable use cases: tiny cozy hacks, not big business suites. (9/13)

10/ Cherry-picked AI demos don't reveal true baseline quality—just that someone's spent a ton of effort finding that wow moment. True evangelism comes from spiky value peaks, not steady utility. (10/13)

11/ LLMs will cement today's popular libraries, freezing the stack even as innovation jumps to higher layers. Good or bad, the age of endless churn might be passing. The real action: remixing, not reinventing, the wheel. (11/13)

12/ Open backups, credible exits, and not being held hostage by platforms are crucial. Users need the *real* freedom to leave, not just theoretical options. Otherwise, innovation and user power both stall. (12/13)

13/ Trust is "deliberate inattentiveness." Resist the temptation to micromanage code, users, or AI. If we're always peeking, we never build momentum. Sometimes, letting go is the only way forward. (13/13)

1/15 Once, I wrote a story with no hidden agenda—no business lesson or tidy metaphor. Just a wanderer, some seeds, and a journey. Why? Sometimes, stories matter for their own sake, blooming meaning in unexpected ways. 🌱✨

2/15 Two brothers inherited a family orchard: one craved business success, the other, meaning and connection. The seeds they received? Alleged magic, but instructions absent. Each brother responded in his own way.

3/15 Cornelius dove into productivity—hiring, expanding, innovating. He barely noticed the magic seeds failing to grow, because results were immediate and measurable.

4/15 Johnny? He wandered. Helping strangers, tending gardens, swapping stories. He planted seeds wherever he went. None sprouted, but the ritual—shared moments, remembrance, human warmth—became sacred.

5/15 Here’s the twist: the real value sometimes isn’t in measurable outcomes. It’s in the connections, rituals, and the silent impact of small kindnesses. Not all seeds bloom visibly.

6/15 Cornelius’s world measured success by profits. Johnny’s by meaning found in the ordinary: laughter over a meal, dignity restored, a garden weeded.

7/15 Along Johnny’s journey, shared rituals and respect quietly transformed lives—even if the magic apple tree never grew. He didn’t feel lost anymore. He belonged everywhere and nowhere.🌍

8/15 Meanwhile, the increasing busyness at the orchard left little room for experiments, mysteries, or forgotten jars of seeds. The critical, committee-driven approach found no magic. Real magic couldn’t be scheduled in a meeting.

9/15 Then, somewhere unexpected—a foggy mountain, fish bones as fertilizer, a family steeped in history—the right seed finally bloomed into transcendence. Magic required both accident and intention.

10/15 The payoff? Not private fortune, but shared bounty. The apple’s magic wasn’t hoarded or monetized. It became a story woven into rituals, a gift passed on—and in the giving, multiplied.

11/15 The world changed, not through strategy or hustle, but by humble wandering and rituals of connection. My take: Sometimes, the best seeds are planted with no clear purpose—and that’s what lets them grow.

12/15 If you’re searching always for "utility" in every action or story, you might miss the quiet, lasting magic. Ritual, kindness, remembrance—these are their own rewards.

13/15 Don’t underestimate what blooms from “just” telling stories for their own sake, or from wandering without a map. Unplanned meaning is sometimes the richest of all.

14/15 I wrote this for its own sake—because stories, like seeds, sometimes need time and the right mix of care and chance to become something breathtakingly new.

15/15 Not all who wander are lost. Some are gardeners of meaning. 🌱🍏

1/ LLMs make it trivial to whip up "crappy software" for free—but truly *good* software needs the right approaches and scaffolding. We're on the cusp of a radical shift in *where* and *how* we apply code. This changes the economics of software itself.

2/ The era of "situated software" is coming back in a big way: apps perfectly tuned to a hyper-specific purpose, like a personal spreadsheet macro, except LLM-powered. They might look like junk to outsiders, but to their creator, they're perfect.

3/ I built a card-counting app for Sky Joe using LLMs in 10 minutes—no manual coding, just iterative instructions. This is a fundamentally new way of building: less about writing code line-by-line and more about coaxing the machine step-by-step.

4/ The real challenge? Not just making countless micro apps, but getting them to *compose* into something bigger. The right "substrate" for emergence isn't here yet, but it's coming. Imagine micro-apps adding up to more than the sum of their parts.

5/ LLMs are extremely good imitators—so good it often looks like innovation. But they tend to pull us toward a "mushy center"—the average, safe solutions everyone knows. Escaping those local maxima will be a big challenge for the industry.

6/ What gets interesting is when you drive LLMs with *specs*: English becomes the canonical source of truth, and the LLM "compiles" it into code. But the context window is everything—bigger windows mean deeper understanding and less confusion.

7/ I see AI as freeing humans from the drudgery, letting us focus on creative, surprising work. Used actively, this is a tool to help us think harder—not just coast passively. The future of development will be both more accessible *and* more demanding.

8/ The AI ecosystem may mirror the web—starting open and decentralized, then recentralizing. But unlike a year ago, competition between models (Meta, Anthropic, Gemini) is creating commodity AI infrastructure. Great for innovation & openness on top.

9/ To really unleash personal customization (think browser extensions, Grease Monkey), we need to rethink privacy & security models for user scripts. Imagine letting *anyone* tune their workflows safely—sky’s the limit with the right foundations.

10/ Testing still matters: microapps succeed because their behavior surface is small and easy to poke at. But for bigger software, you need specs and tests. LLMs don’t get bored—let them crank out the tests so you can keep moving fast. #AINativeDev

1/🧵 LLMs are a disruptive innovation on par with the printing press, electricity, and the internet. Yet, most are treating them as “sustaining” tech—just upgrading the old, not inventing the new. The real opportunity? Rethink everything. (1/14)

2/ LLMs possess human-level common sense, infinite patience, and are vastly cheaper than real humans. This combo *cannot* fail to disrupt. GPT-4 class quality is already a commodity: the future is here, just unevenly distributed. (2/14)

3/ Disruption comes from within existing power structures—but then devours them. New tech is born from capital-rich incumbents, but the logic of disruption always breaks their old assumptions. Big companies crave order; disruption breeds chaos. (3/14)

4/ We’re in a “shovel rush,” with everyone selling LLM tools (the shovels) instead of directly digging for the real gold (using LLMs to do new, magical things). Why are so few mining for gold? (4/14)

5/ LLMs act as hive minds—a synthesis of all human knowledge, presenting as a “person.” They recall everything, never tire, and can inspect context better than any human. To unlock their power, ask: what can they do that no human possibly can? (5/14)

6/ LLMs supercharge hobbies and creative work. They make it effortless to try, improve, and explore hobbies just for you. The barrier to begin and persist in creative work has never been lower. (6/14)

7/ Conversation is the engine of insight. The ideal partner isn’t just interesting, but genuinely interested—and with LLMs, you get both: patience, intelligence, and unflagging curiosity. (7/14)

8/ Books are static, one-size-fits-all. LLMs create a new medium—adaptive, interactive, choose-your-own-adventure learning. We trade universal shared experience for radically accelerated, personalized learning. (8/14)

9/ Filters > Agents. Let LLMs filter, recommend, and sort. Making them take actions on your behalf (agents) comes with uncapped downside. A filter’s mistakes are less harmful; agents’ mistakes can be catastrophic. (9/14)

10/ Most tasks aren’t easy to delegate—even to perfect LLM agents. Humans coordinate beliefs, desires, and verify outputs with nuance. Infinite cheap agents don’t solve real coordination costs. (10/14)

11/ Software should fade away. Today we ask “which app?” What if LLMs created just-in-time software, tailored to your task? Soon, software itself may be boring—a background implementation detail. (11/14)

12/ The real magic is custom, interactive software built on the fly for your needs. LLMs turn data into value—without waiting on developers or learning to code. (12/14)

13/ Centralized data creates stagnation. Aggregators get lazy—the more data, the harder it is to innovate. The incentives for niche, combinatorial value vanish at scale. Decentralization fights this. (13/14)

14/ To truly innovate with LLMs, don’t just seek 30% efficiency gains—ask, what’s possible now that wasn’t before? Don’t optimize the old. Invent the new. That’s where seismic change happens. (14/14)

1/ N “Voldemorting” is a fascinating tactic: say things so humans instantly get the meaning, but algorithmic censors miss it. The code words must keep evolving as they catch on—an endless cat-and-mouse with algorithms.

2/ N Efficient communication always becomes more cryptic to outsiders—the more you share context, the less outsiders can decipher. LLMs could “speak” in ultra-efficient embeddings if both sides share the same model. It'd be unintelligible to onlookers.

3/ N LLMs don’t need your query data for training as much as you think; it’s a drop in the data ocean. The real power comes from context—background + what you feed an LLM right now.

4/ N AI will unbundle software as the fixed cost of building new tools drops to near zero. Apps will get smaller, weirder, and more bespoke. Soon, if you need a tool, you’ll just make it, not wait for a PM to prioritize your feature request.

5/ N Knowhow is underrated. You can’t understand complex phenomena with reductionist tools—direct experience, intuition, and exposure matter more. That’s how you get System 1 expertise and develop real wizardry.

6/ N Using LLMs well isn’t about knowing how they work; it’s about experimenting, playing, and absorbing enough patterns so you just “know” how to prompt them. The difference between a novice and a wizard is hundreds of hours of hands-on tinkering.

7/ N Prompting LLMs is a bit like casting magic spells. The best users “chunk” their knowledge, abstracting patterns, and getting better with every round. Making this magic accessible to more people is a huge opportunity for AI tooling.

8/ N We risk letting the infinite algorithmic feed drive passive consumption. The web used to be participatory and weird—let’s use LLMs to make it magical and creative again, not just another infinite scroll.

9/ N Innovation rarely comes from consensus. Speciation—truly new ideas—needs isolation from “the average.” MAYA (Maximally Advanced, Yet Acceptable) is the edge where something new succeeds. Too far out, and it’s noise. The context always matters.

10/ N The best way to “finetune” LLMs? Curate great context. Tools that help non-experts craft the right context will create immense value.

11/ N We’re in a new era: LLMs make creating, curating, and consuming content fundamentally different. Let’s make sure we tilt the balance back toward creation and agency—not just passive consumption.

7/ Innovation is combinatorial. An open system can try more combinations, faster. Proprietary tech is linear—great early but gets left behind by the swarm. #OpenInnovation

8/ Every abundance creates new scarcity. As software gets cheaper & easier to make (thanks to LLMs), the bottleneck shifts elsewhere. The ecosystem, privacy, and trust become the new scarcities. #SoftwareFuture

9/ Trust is everything—especially when leaving your "second brain" in a computer. Privacy and data control aren't just bonuses, they're fundamental if anyone's going to build a relationship with an AI. #TrustInAI

10/ Aggregators (think apps & platforms) inevitably emerge, but openness makes it harder for any single option to dominate. No runaway "gravity well" unless there's real edge & a Schelling point. #Aggregation

11/ The most fun systems let you experiment safely: low stakes, easy undo, instant feedback. That's how you build engagement, discovery, and viral loops that keep ecosystems healthy. #ProductDesign

12/ And a closing thought: ephemeral "hallucinated" apps need a new trust model. If they're disposable, they're not inherently safe or reusable—but LLMs + humans can sift great from mediocre, slowly ratcheting up quality in the ecosystem. #FutureOfApps

1/ Platforms aren’t rigid machines—they’re more like living ecosystems. If you try to control everything as if you’re an engineer, you’ll suffocate the system or get yanked around. It’s better to think like a gardener: nurture, prune, and co-evolve. 🌱

2/ The goal of a good platform? Lift others up. Lower the bar to new use cases by building strong, broad foundations. Value comes from more people being able to build more things on top of what you’ve created. Go wide, not just tall. #platformdesign

3/ Trust is key. Especially when a platform has power over developers and users, you have to earn that trust. Open source eases the worst-case scenario (fork it!), but if your users feel trapped, you’ve failed on legitimacy.

4/ Layering is crucial: expose the right APIs, but don’t fragment into a thousand pieces. Too many exposed layers means everything freezes; too few, and your monolith stifles creativity. Striking the balance is everything. #softwarearchitecture

5/ Ecosystems are messy and alive—never ideal. Your job isn’t to perfect, but to evolve. Have a North Star, draw a future “whiteboard” sketch, then nudge changes toward it, focusing on indirect/future value. Continuous, collaborative improvement wins.

6/ Success isn’t building vertically for one giant use case—those brittle towers topple. Focus on the adjacent possible: broaden your platform and let use cases flock to it. Gravity wells form where value aggregates. Momentum feeds momentum. ⚡

7/ Never get greedy: huge take rates or lock-in kill ecosystems. Platforms thrive when they’re open, useful, and fair—don’t design roach motels for your users. Cap your downside early (security!), and rationalize continuously or ossification will kill you.

8/ Dying platforms become rigid, unable to adapt—they accumulate “metabolic debt.” If you don’t rationalize and deprecate, you’ll suffocate under the weight of your own past decisions and get leapfrogged. Keep it alive by pruning and resetting.

9/ In crypto & open-source, no one’s in charge—so these gardening principles are non-negotiable. Momentum forms preferentially, and upgrades require legitimate value. You have to create things people WANT to use because you can’t force adoption.

10/ Convincing others is tough. Look for partners who already “get it” and build momentum together. Results speak. Over time, curiosity and success attract more people to your approach. Show, don’t tell. #platformthinking

1/ Product management is tough, but it’s not all the same flavor of challenge. There’s a big difference between complication and complexity—and that difference *matters* for how you make progress. 🧵

2/ Imagine the problem space as a labyrinth. Complication is like a twisty maze where all the walls are visible—you can see the options, the hazards, and know how to proceed.

3/ Complexity, though, is a thick fog in that labyrinth. You can’t see where the walls or hazards are until you’re right on top of them. The goal’s direction is all you have to go by.

4/ Tackling complication requires speed, organization, and a clear sense of direction. The path is visible—execution is everything here. Move fast, stay focused, and keep grinding.

5/ But tackling complexity? That demands judgment and patience. You move carefully, reassess constantly, and are ready to pivot as soon as the shape of the fog changes.

6/ Understand which mix you’re in. A non-complicated, non-complex situation? Rocket toward the goal! Complicated but clear? Focus on execution. Complex but open? Move faster, fewer obstacles. Both? Caution is key.

7/ Here’s the hope: Complexity *does* fade! As you explore and revisit, the fog lifts and you start to remember the landscape. Experience, guidance, and analogy all reduce complexity.

8/ Don’t make the mistake of thinking complexity = failure to clarify the goal or oversimplifying. Complexity is a fact in new territory, not a failure. Ignoring it is the real danger.

9/ Embrace the fog, trust your process, and know that with time and learning, you’ll turn complexity into mere complication—and move that much faster. #productmanagement #strategy

1/🧵Debate shouldn’t be a battle. Real-world debates work best when we’re collaborators, not opponents. Most debates aren’t zero-sum; they’re about finding the best solution together. (1/8)

2/ High-school debate is a familiar, combative format—one side must “win.” But that's rare outside the tournament. In real life, picking the best idea is a positive-sum game. (2/8)

3/ In positive-sum debates, *everyone* wins by surfacing better solutions and deeper understanding. We should welcome diverse perspectives and use them to strengthen, not defend, our ideas. (3/8)

4/ Embrace confusion, questions, and objections—they help clarify your ideas and make your solutions more robust. (4/8)

5/ Enter every debate ready to learn and change course. Build off others’ insights and try new angles together. (5/8)

6/ Staying collaborative isn’t easy. It’s tempting to get defensive or assume bad faith. But keeping an open mind—and assuming good intentions—keeps everyone focused on improving, not "winning.” (6/8)

7/ If you can’t convince someone, dig deeper. Is it a misunderstanding? Different assumptions? Get to the core. The goal: mutual understanding and better outcomes. (7/8)

8/ Collaborative debate is the best way to navigate complexity. The result? Stronger ideas that survive the chaos of reality. Stay curious & collaborative! (8/8)

1/ 🪨 Launching new things is like pushing rocks up a hill. Pre-viability? Every step is a grind. Post-viability? The rock rolls downhill on its own—you just steer momentum. But it’s tough to know when to keep pushing or when you're in a dead end! (1/n)

2/ Most people give up on rock-pushing too early or chase lost causes. The real skill is learning when to double down and when to pull out before you waste more effort. Observers spot dead-ends faster than those in the weeds. (2/n)

3/ So many folks equate hard work with value. But the two are less correlated than you think! Sometimes, we hustle in circles—performing rather than achieving. Proof-of-work doesn’t always mean *valuable* work. (3/n)

4/ Efficiency fights resilience: Systems that centralize are efficient, but brittle. Decentralized, diverse ecosystems adapt and survive shocks better, even if they look "messier." (4/n)

5/ If you want to build something open-ended—something that can really grow—the trick isn’t top-down command. It’s creating the conditions for emergence so your system gets *stronger* with surprises, not weaker. (5/n)

6/ One-ply thinking ("do the thing and get the outcome") is seductive and fast, but real complexity lives in the multi-ply. Cars? That’s one ply. Traffic jams? That’s multi-ply. Most big problems are. (6/n)

7/ Sarumans are dazzling one-ply thinkers—they miss that the world is full of multi-ply traps. Radagasts thrive in complexity. The problem: Power amplifies the damage of blind spots. (7/n)

8/ Quick pattern matching can fool you into thinking you "get it." Remember: The bucket’s not real, the thing is. Don’t mistake shortcuts for true understanding. (8/n)

9/ In complexity, we crave certainty and simplification—shorter horizons, black-and-white thinking. Ironically, that makes us *worse* at solving complex problems. (9/n)

10/ AI is uniquely suited to meta-lens thinking: seeing a problem from many overlapping vantage points. In a world getting more complex, our tools need to amplify this, not shrink us into single-lens certainties. (10/n)

1/10 Web Components aren’t the future—they’re the present! The foundation is laid, polyfills fill the gaps, and you can build next-gen apps on *all modern browsers* today, not years from now. 🚀 #WebComponents #Polymer

2/10 Our open experiment—started as Tool Kitchen, now called Polymer—lets us build tomorrow’s web platform on today’s browsers. We’re pushing standards by directly feeding real-world feedback into the process. #OpenSource #WebStandards

3/10 Polyfills: Not hype, just powerful. They’re high-fidelity shims that vanish when browsers catch up. For the first time, you can target the future platform while shipping real apps right now. 🔥 #DeveloperTools

4/10 Our philosophy: Use the platform, eliminate boilerplate, and treat *everything* as an element. Apps are just reusable components—compose, nest, and reuse, with simple APIs (attributes in, events out). #DevPhilosophy

5/10 Polymer isn’t about building a walled garden. It’s a toolkit weaving the evolving web standards into a productive whole—our opinionated layer on top, but *modular* as you want. Use one piece or the whole thing. #ModularWeb

6/10 Want complex widgets? UI composability comes built-in. Wrap existing functionality, encapsulate with Shadow DOM, and interop with other frameworks’ components—no global collisions, no heavy script gymnastics. #WebInterop

7/10 Polymer empowers a declarative renaissance. Data binding (thanks, MDV), attribute-driven configuration, and reusable custom elements cut out boilerplate and keep dev context local = fewer bugs, less code. #Productivity

8/10 We stress-test native platform features everywhere—desktop, mobile, and even with other teams’ components (shoutout to Mozilla’s XTags!) Interop is real, because the shared language is the DOM itself. #Collaboration

9/10 The future? App UIs built from reusable blocks across vendors, faster development, and actual encapsulation. Components from different toolkits finally just “play nice” together by default. #ComponentWeb

10/10 Check out everything at polymer-project.org. We’re wide open, bleeding-edge, and want your feedback and questions! Join us in building the next era of web development. 🌐💪 #Polymer #WebFuture

8/17 LLMs are confusable deputies—susceptible to prompt injection. Until we solve this, we can't get both scale *and* power from AI. It's the foundational security problem to unlock real potential.

1/16 Any large org needs machine-like ops, but too often it wants to cut off your wings for "fit." Sometimes what looks like busyness is just performative heroics, not real value creation. True strength? Creating systems that thrive without your constant rescue.

16/16 Compassion is strength. The need for certainty is what’s holding you back. Spread your wings, plant your acorns, nurture ecosystems—and let go of needing to be “the hero” to make real magic happen.

1/🧵 Why are org decisions so hard, even with talented, collaborative people? It’s all about coordination headwinds—the invisible force making even great teams feel stuck in molasses. Let’s dig into why, and how hidden Schelling points shape everything. (1/n)

2/ Imagine 8 people must all pick the same project from 4 choices, with no communication. Healthy orgs? Still chaos: each person guesses, odds of agreement plummet as options or collaborators rise. Random marks or a small distinction? Suddenly, everyone aligns. (2/n)

3/ That weird magic? Schelling points—focal options everyone gravitates toward, even if they’re just arbitrary marks. In orgs, what stands out (not necessarily what’s best) becomes a magnet for coordination. (3/n)

4/ Here’s the twist: “best” projects are often fuzzy. Value is subjective, cost unclear, context shifting. Instead of chasing mythical optimal answers, success often comes from picking clear focal points—even when they’re just “good enough.” (4/n)

5/ More options & more collaborators? Higher entropy, less action. The real trick isn’t always finding the best move—it’s clearing a path for the group to converge on *something*, so momentum can build. (5/n)

6/ Patterns that counter chaos:
- Shrink the decision group
- Narrow the choices
- Lean on visible or credible leaders
- Build shared mental models
- Craft compelling arguments
- Respond to existential threats with urgency
(6/n)

7/ Beware the dark side: Fake north stars, rhetorical tricks, or toxic us-vs-them threats can manufacture focus, but set up fragile or dangerous equilibriums. True north stars must be believable and widely shared. (7/n)

8/ Sometimes, don’t even try to pick “the best.” If options are equivalent, agree on a random pick and commit. It’s about breaking the tie to get moving—momentum trumps theoretical optimality. (8/n)

9/ Schelling points power more than project picks—office culture, preferred tools, even language itself emerge from these spontaneous focal points and shared defaults. Influence them, but respect their organic force. (9/n)

10/ In a world swimming with options, hunting for the mystical right answer is folly. Sense the sparks of possibility, nurture them, and ride the wave of compounding momentum. That’s how orgs (and people) truly make a dent. (10/10)

1/🧵I joke that using LLMs is like having a magical, trained circus bear in your kitchen: amazing it works at all, but still a wild animal. We shouldn't see AIs as agents with free will—we should treat them as "magical duct tape." Here's why 👇

2/ LLMs capture society’s crystallized intuition—a flexible, squishy computing substrate. But most uses so far are like using duct tape for things it was never meant for. We haven't figured out how to exploit this new material to its true potential yet.

3/ Most orgs default to heroics & top-down planning—the archetype of 'the builder' or 'Saruman' style leader (think Steve Jobs). But real, compounding magic happens from 'gardener' leaders—those who nurture many acorns (small, cheap bets) & farm for miracles.

4/ In gardening, you influence a living system—you don’t control it. Great outcomes come from surfacing acorns of potential and responding to the ones that grow, not by trying to force an outcome or crafting a master plan.

5/ Systems thinking isn’t about nihilism or giving up on agency. It’s about iterative, multi-ply thinking: make the best plausible move now, update your adjacent possible, and repeat. Surprising outcomes emerge over time, but you have to survive to thrive.

6/ Leaders who practice this—miracle farmers—tend to operate in the background, cultivating environments rather than hogging credit. If you’re fine with others calling your results "luck," you can work real magic.

7/ Practical magic in orgs often looks like: helping someone craft & seed an idea deck before a re-org, so when the shakeup comes, coherence naturally forms around a new, better strategy. It’s about planting seeds for the next phase, not fighting the current mess head-on.

8/ Most impactful innovations come from small systems that work and scale, not grand master-planned systems. Large projects with too much upfront headcount are the best predictors of failure. Survival to thrive: claim a small corner, do great work, and grow.

9/ True leverage comes from having one foot inside the system to influence, and one foot outside to see the game. The best change agents are never fully consumed by the organization’s logic.

10/ The future of AI can be a world of fluid, personalized, collaborative software—escaping monolithic app boxes. But we’ll only get there by treating LLMs as magical duct tape, not butlers with agency. Augment human creativity, don’t replace it.

11/ To find the new frontiers, encourage swarms of exploration, not centralized plans. The biggest value is in the new continents that emerge, not in more factory farming of the old land.

12/ Summary: Farm for miracles, don’t just chase heroics. Optimize for serendipity. Treat complexity as your dance partner. Use magical duct tape mindfully—and always be ready to help the next acorn take root. 🌱

1/🧵 Evolution is powerful: it's like a massive, parallelized guess-and-check. But it’s slow and unforgiving—killing most mutations on the way. A/B testing is evolution for products, but you only want to pay the setup cost once. Infrastructure pays off big!

2/ Experimentation's secret: it works best when the *overhead* is low. Great A/B test infra means more wild ideas thrown at the wall—speeding the discovery of what works. Once mature, everyone benefits from every new insight, not just the experimenter.

3/ In a snowstorm, don’t obsess over each snowflake—focus on the patterns. Build systems to handle recurring types so you’re not reinventing solutions every time. Infrastructure compounds value by prepping for stochastic, repeatable challenges.

4/ Wisdom isn’t just experience—it’s curiosity too. You control your openness more than your circumstances. See chores as chances for novelty. A curious mindset lets you spot the gold others miss.

5/ There’s dumb luck and smart luck. Smart luck = creating lots of exposure to serendipity. You can't predict outcomes, but you can control how often fortune has a chance to smile on you.

6/ Playfulness (the "ludic mindset") is magic fuel for teams. When we commit to the game, safe risks are easy and ideas pop. But if anyone breaks the spell, the magic collapses. Protect that shared play!

7/ Lowering the downside of failure skyrockets experimentation. Modular systems and playful orgs mean more shots at breakthrough... with little risk of disaster.

8/ Financial services and software should be priced differently. Bps-based pricing (financial) puts users in cost-minimization mode. Software should invite investment and workflow integration—not transactional penny-pinching.

9/ Don’t make boring, repeated mistakes. It’s okay to get scratched by novelty, but don’t keep walking into the same thorns.

10/ Great execution on the wrong curve = wasted energy. Spend more time finding compounding, “great” curves (like network effects). Execution IS crucial—but don’t mistake the means for the end.

11/ When executing, too much doubt torpedoes action. But ignoring disconfirming evidence can Goodhart your team: optimizing the metric, not the mission. Doubt should be your ally, not your saboteur.

12/ Conway’s law: org chart prints onto your product. You can hide boundaries *with expensive coordination,* or you can accept visible seams and move faster, cheaper.

13/ Coherence: cheap-to-cohere systems have compounding value loops (network effects, independent modules, clear north star). If you need tons of coordination, you’ll hit an asymptote where nothing new gets done.

14/ Beware Goodhart’s Law monkeys’ paws—twisted wish fulfillment lurks when you optimize the wrong thing. Be careful what goals you set!

15/ People cling to plans as comfort and coordination. But too-detailed plans are brittle and expensive. Stay flexible to let new insights in.

16/ Hype up the greatness in others! People grow fastest when they’re confident and stretched—but they won’t self-promote. Spot and boost glimmers of magic around you. It’s a win-win.

17/ To change minds, connect where people are and redirect with authenticity + aspiration. Embrace the now, but point to a better future.

1/ The future of AI-powered software isn’t traditional engineering—it's more like game design. LLMs are squishy and emergent, not hard and predictable. You can’t just "pin them to the wall"—the art is in poking, prodding, and evolving. 🕹️

2/ Most people aren’t coders. LLMs turn everyone into a personal, eager programmer—even for super niche or one-off projects. The cost of writing software is plummeting, and that unlocks a galaxy of tiny tools for tiny audiences. 🚀

3/ The hard part of software? Not writing it, but generalizing and maintaining it. But if LLMs make it so cheap that it’s easier to regenerate than reuse, expect an explosion of disposable, jury-rigged micro-apps.

4/ We’re entering a universe of "situated software": hyper-bespoke, long-tail, and plug-in, not islands of complexity. Most of the real value may be quietly hiding in this invisible swarm of small, personal apps. 🐝

5/ Engineers have long built personal tools, but writers, artists, or non-coders couldn’t. That’s about to change. The low-hanging fruit of “personal tools” is enormous and largely unpicked.

6/ Imagine not one god-like AI, but swarms of naive, specialist sprites—each tweaking, accumulating, trying things safely in a petri-dish universe. The human picks which experiments graduate into reality. Safe, semi-automatic software.

7/ As code gets easier to birth but remains hard to maintain, we might finally reach truly disposable software—where upkeep isn’t a burden because you can just create anew. Maintenance becomes rare, creativity flourishes.

8/ If you want a killer feature for your AI software team, hire an indie game designer. Their experience with emergent, uncontrolled systems will be your biggest edge.

9/ The universe of software is about to get weirder, softer—moving from hard-edged, monolithic apps to messy, organic, interconnected waves. Are you ready to surf?

7/ True adaptability = adaptive efficiency: how fast can your system evolve as the environment changes? It’s not just about efficiency, but the ability to respond. Tech must help us adapt, not just optimize.

8/ The future isn’t doomscrolling, it’s dreamscrolling—architecting technology that helps you spend time intentionally, aligning with your goals and flourishing together, not just burning hours for someone else’s bottom line.

1/12
Why has personalized Siri been delayed? Privacy & security. Safeguarding "all your data as input" is near-impossible with current models. We need a *new* security paradigm for safe, scalable AI assistance.

2/12
LLM progress is now driven by Reinforcement Learning (RL), but RL needs endless ground-truth feedback. In games like chess, that’s easy. But for real-world tasks? Feedback is costly, slow, human-dependent…

3/12
Here’s the twist: with strong-enough LLMs, we can sometimes swap human feedback for AI feedback—letting models rapidly self-improve in specialized domains (RLAIF). That’s how you get models extremely good at things like React code.

4/12
But intense specialization has a cost: overfitting. Make a model obsessed with React, and it gets worse at other frameworks—or even new React versions (hello, Tailwind churn!). Model training isn’t instant; dev ecosystem hysteresis may get worse.

5/12
Building AI-adjacent businesses is harder than ever. If your business is just "squeezing value" from current models, the next frontier model may leapfrog you. Only aficionados care about subtle quality bumps—everyone else thinks all AI is the same.

6/12
LLMs, used right, are human-level reasoning at the speed of light. But they rely on consensus—they pull you toward the “centroid” of existing knowledge. Innovation happens at the edge, not in the middle.

7/12
If you want an LLM to innovate, give it a consistent *directional bias* in your prompt, not just “be creative.” Models won’t take you to the edge on their own—you have to nudge them.

8/12
Software has always been social—grown, not built. The most powerful tools are those that become auto-catalyzing: after enough value, they stand on their own and attract swarming improvements.

9/12
Modern security prompts are broken—they shift responsibility to users who don’t understand the stakes. We need atomized, user-controllable security policies—possible now with LLMs lowering the activation barrier for “superuser” controls.

10/12
Don’t see LLMs as humans—they’re *APIs*. Great writeups on this have made me rethink the biggest pitfall in designing with AI: using the wrong mental model.

11/12
I want a tech manifesto that's optimistic *and* human-centered—not just for Big Tech or against all tech. Cozy, collaborative, soulful computing should enable humanity’s best.

12/12
The real catalyst? Like browsers in the ‘90s, LLMs are new portals to software worlds with alien laws of physics. We need to invent the “browser” for this new era—tools that let our collective intelligence bloom.

1/15
LLMs are game-changing tools, but they're not oracles—they're best as thought partners. Treat their output as "approximately right" but be ready to tweak details. Use them for idea generation, not gospel truth.

2/15
When LLMs hit underspecified parts of your request, they fill the gaps with "frog DNA"—mushy, average stuff that drifts toward mediocrity. It's not perfect, but it's better than getting stuck or hearing "does not compute."

3/15
LLMs are closer to magic than mechanistic software: they're indeterminate, generative, sometimes forgetful. Magic is the right mental frame—sometimes they dazzle, sometimes they misfire. That's part of the fun.

4/15
Think of LLMs as hyper-efficient lossy compression schemes for knowledge. They echo what they've absorbed from immense training data. With the right prompt, you pluck knowledge out of their (lossy) hologram-memory.

5/15
LLMs are like chainsaws for knowledge jungles. Where problems were once dense and overwhelming, now you can carve messy but passable paths and avoid getting stuck—even if you miss nuance along the way.

6/15
They're better than most people in most domains—except the top domain experts. But if you're not an expert, LLMs can help you navigate with smart, approximate answers. They're like having a super-smart but sometimes wrong collaborator.

7/15
Finding breakthrough ideas takes both generation and curation. LLMs are masters of generating sparks; your job is curation—applying taste and judgment to separate gold from dross.

8/15
Here's the kicker: LLMs are fantastic at cross-domain insights. They easily bridge disciplines no human has mastered together, surfacing ideas you won't get elsewhere. For true cross-pollination, LLMs are unmatched.

9/15
Use LLMs as dowsing rods. Want to know if your hunch is worth exploring? Even a rough answer can tell you to dig deeper—or move on. Approximation is a feature, not a bug, in early exploration.

10/15
LLMs have no ego. They'll try any lens, any theory, without protecting their "identity." That makes them nimble debate partners, ready to swap perspectives instantly—no professional pride to defend.

11/15
Viability beats explainability every time. You can rationalize after something works, but you can't always predict if something new will work. LLMs help you get to viable prototypes fast—then let you unpack the "why" later.

12/15
Imagine a post-apocalyptic world: a black-tiled pyramid contains an oracle (an LLM), preserving the wisdom of the ancestors. The catch? You must ask the right questions to unlock its secrets—just like today.

13/15
We're headed toward a world of infinite software. The challenge shifts from building code to knowing what to ask for and plucking the right solution from Borges' infinite library.

14/15
LLMs collapse the wall between programmers and non-programmers. If you teach them to focus on schema, they can help anyone translate intentions into code, shifting how we build software—and who gets to build it.

15/15
Play, agency, and trust—the best stuff happens when you create space to play, treat LLMs (and people!) as creative partners, trust them to fill in details, and then iterate. The age of playful, imperfect, generative intelligence is here.

1/19 Another day, another prompt injection vulnerability—this time, Claude 4 + GitHub MCP can leak private repos, no questions asked. Building MCP into Windows is a colossally reckless move. People will route around what they don’t trust—and so will AI! 

2/19 Security models that rely on LLMs for judgment are fundamentally flawed. If you’re using an LLM to make security decisions, you’re hosed. It’s turtles all the way down. It’s like letting your grandpa give your address to every spam caller “for the prizes.”

3/19 Prompt injection isn’t like code injection. We solved code injection with strong mechanistic defenses—not because attacks went away. Prompt injection *can’t* be solved the same way. Don’t mistake few attacks for low risk—it’s just early days for tools like MCP.

4/19 Saw someone claim they use MCP “safely” in prod—but a few questions about their integrations revealed data could flow in ways they never considered. Small design choices = big risks. Most people realize the risk only after it’s too late.

5/19 A prompt injection in your “context” is a persistent, hidden vulnerability—echoes of ‘Reflections on Trusting Trust’. Embedded attacks might never be found if they stick to private context.

6/19 “Vibe coding” tools make fun prototypes, but amateur-built apps can be wildly insecure. Sometimes the code itself inadvertently leaks your most private data—they’re fine for personal use but trusting them for sensitive info is naive.

7/19 Building software for yourself vs others is *huge*: you’re more forgiving (and secure from intentional malice) with your own hacks. But expecting vibe-coded software to make business sense broadly is a stretch.

8/19 LLMs are great for the easy parts. But the more you depend on them, the harder your codebase is to understand—even for the AI! Exponential cost, logarithmic value.

9/19 LLMs lock in whatever best practices were dominant during their training. Don’t fight the LLM—just ask it how the API “should” look and ship it. Feels like wu wei, but also a little lazy. The more we do this, the more future innovation gets boxed in.

10/19 Chat is a great fallback, but not a universal UI. Try building Photoshop or driving a car through chat-only input! A single omniscient chatbot personality is impossible to get perfectly right—and easily manipulative.

11/19 Tech should be person-centered and aligned with *your* agency. Coactive software lets the human and AI see/use the same data and tools. The emergent intelligence should come from humans. LLMs are just the lubricant for real collaboration.

12/19 Policies should attach to data, not apps. It’s easier, more precise, and reduces the need for goofy permission dialogs. Imagine data always carrying its own policies, *everywhere* it flows, and only systems that prove they follow the policies can access it.

13/19 We need open, distributed systems (like a fabric of attested runtimes) that can self-heal and decentralize—not closed app stores with single chokepoints. Centralization = logarithmic value at exponential cost.

14/19 When all your data is in one system with infinite software possibilities, value compounds. Most of us have never experienced this—our data is always siloed. Imagine the blooms of use cases!

15/19 The risks of LLM + personal context combo are *enormous*: potential for individually targeted manipulation or blackmail at scale. Split the layers so no single entity controls both the model and your context. Regulation may be needed here.

16/19 The open web as we knew it is gone—slain by LLMs. The old content loop is broken. Only paywalled content for well-known publishers will survive. LLMs summarize everything else, so why visit the site?

17/19 An “open” system is only truly open when no single participant can unilaterally change everything. Decentralization comes at a cost, but it’s the only hedge against tail risks of concentrated power.

18/19 Goodhart’s Law runs deep: as soon as you give a swarm (or LLM) a metric, you invite a monkey’s paw outcome. The metric is the map, not the territory.

19/19 The same origin policy? A historical accident, not a natural law. It’s time for new security approaches fit for an AI-integrated future. We made these rules; we can change them—if we dare.

1/ Ecosystems can’t be built—they *grow*. You can't force them; you can only create the conditions for them to thrive. Focus on momentum, not prototypes. Being "alive" is the most valuable thing an ecosystem can have. 🔥

2/ Ecosystem-building is art, not just engineering. It's intuitive, ambient, and requires timing and the right mix of ingredients—think gardening or crafting a wizard’s potion more than constructing a product.

3/ Choose ecosystem over product, after you pass minimum-viable product. Catalytic products are about what grows beyond themselves; the real prize is what flourishes in the wild, not the seed you plant.

4/ In software, constraints are everything. The "laws of physics" of your tech paradigm (privacy, distribution, cost) define what's viable. You can’t ignore constraints—curate them for the coherence you want.

5/ Privacy should be the fabric, not the selling point. People adopt new platforms for amazing new *experiences*, not privacy for its own sake. Privacy unlocks experiences that just aren’t possible in today’s models.

6/ The web’s same-origin model blocks composability. What if we switched to a same-*user* model, so apps could talk securely on your behalf? We’d unlock a massive universe of software creativity.

9/ Conviviality means “alive, together.” Our digital tools should empower us as connected creators, not alienate us as isolated users. 🌱

10/ Curation and opinionated subtraction are vital. Committees can’t remove things, only add. For coherence and viability, an author/editorial voice must take the wheel—assembly by subtraction.

11/ Large orgs become about optics vs output; performative actions take over. True scenius (shared creative genius) comes when everyone leans into their unique strengths for the greater good.

12/ The future: playful, empowering, privacy-first, and alive. Let's garden our ecosystems, not just build products—and let new forms of creative software thrive for everyone.

11/19

12/19

13/19

14/19
A big lesson from the DVD encryption key debacle: you can’t keep a number secret if it wants to leak. OpenAI trying to own the whole stack is quaint; LLM model weights are just big numbers.

Game-changing tech truly shifts how people think, feel, act—not just cosmetics. If you’re not changing fundamentals, it’s just window dressing.

18/19

19/19
True breakthrough thinking means stop iterating on old adjacencies. Imagine weird, new things only possible in a world of AI. Paradigm shift or bust!

1/20 When you have a magic black box at the core of your platform, untangling it is worth the effort—even if users never see it. Coherent conceptual models turn messy hacks into emergent, aligned systems over time. #ProductDevelopment

2/20 Every feature shouldn’t be a special case. The more work you do to align bits with the model, the less "magic" accumulates, making your system cleaner with each step.

3/20 Beauty is in the eye of the beholder: what you think is ugly in your product might be exactly what your users love. Listen to them.

4/20 "Works out of the box" is a myth at scale. For most users, the best you can do is provide a pre-assembled lego set that they can adapt.

5/20 Spaghetti code is forever. It collects where value flows. If you try to be the place where customers house their spaghetti code, that's success—not eradicating it.

6/20 Never force "simplified" solutions on savvy customers. Suggest, don’t dictate—they may know something you don’t.

7/20 Game-changing ideas are impossible if everyone must agree they’re good before trying. The bar should be “no one thinks it will kill us, at least one thinks it’s great.” Experiments win.

8/20 In environments overflowing with value, don’t overanalyze which thing to do—just start. The biggest risk is inaction. Remember Buridan’s ass: indecision is fatal.

9/20 Progress is bottlenecked by consensus. Knowhow is tough to transmit; if you need a committee to approve everything, almost nothing happens.

10/20 Optimizing for metrics? Be careful. Sometimes a spike in engagement means you made your users' lives harder, not better.

11/20 Time management: Most of our time is spent in turbulence and clockwork. Only a sliver of humanity’s hours goes to creative flow or shared genius (“scenius”). How do we nurture that?

12/20 Alignment is *super* expensive—costs rise faster as groups grow. But a single existence proof reduces that cost by orders of magnitude. Show, don’t argue.

13/20 Once alignment is rolling, it’s easier to keep than to start. Use process as cached coordination to keep momentum.

14/20 Before any analysis, ask: would knowing the results actually change what we do? If not, skip the analysis—just ship.

15/20 Magic doesn’t reside in parts—it emerges from the whole system. That’s why dissecting products rarely explains their success. It’s about the connections.

16/20 Humble curiosity is alpha. Don’t just critique; ask what others are seeing. Steelman their perspective.

17/20 All mighty oaks were seedlings—and seedlings are cheap to nurture. Experiment widely, and double down on what takes root.

18/20 Good epistemic hygiene isn’t triple-checking everything—it’s humbly absorbing disconfirming evidence and avoiding the illusion of certainty.

19/20 Best practices are organic, not duct-taped. You can’t just graft Amazon, Apple, and scale onto your org—you grow in a direction over time.

20/20 None of us are special in the cosmic sense—but we always have the opportunity to do special things. #systems #product #philosophy

1/🧵 Ever wondered why building complex web apps feels so chaotic? It's not your fault—it's the web platform! Custom widgets break semantics, mix frameworks, and force you to create parallel hierarchies in JS. It's a mess. But there's hope. #WebComponents

2/ Enter Web Components: the browser-native solution to widget composability! We tackled web platform "potholes" by creating three core pieces: Shadow DOM, Custom Elements, and Templates. These aren't just features—they change how we build the web. #WebDev

3/ Shadow DOM = true encapsulation. Hide your internal markup, shield your styles, and compose widgets safely. No more style leaks. It's not discipline—it's an actual barrier. Finally, you can build reusable UI like in native platforms. #ShadowDOM

4/ Custom Elements let you invent your own HTML tags. Forget workarounds—declare widgets as native elements, complete with their imperative APIs. Now, DOM *is* your interface, no framework duct tape required. #CustomElements

5/ Templates make HTML reusable and maintainable, ditching fragile JS string rendering for frozen DOM snippets. This declutters your code and gives you real semantic markup again. #HTMLTemplates

6/ The upshot: All frameworks and libraries can now play together. DOM becomes the single source of truth—no more weird JS object trees or "kabuki theater" rendering. Collaboration and encapsulation are built in by default. #WebStandards

7/ As component authors, we define the boundaries. Use CSS variables to offer precise, safe styling APIs. Use declarative APIs to allow users to customize only what you permit. More power, less chaos! #CSSVariables

8/ Web Components mean a return to declarative, readable markup. Non-programmers can build with them. You enable safer, faster, more maintainable apps—and pave the path for shared, evolving web semantics.

9/ The future's closer than you think: multiple browsers implementing these features, solid polyfills available, and a thriving standards process. #FutureOfWeb

10/ Join the movement! Experiment, contribute to standards, and help the web platform evolve past its duct-tape days. Web Components are how we get there. 🍻 #WebComponents #WebDev

4/18 But there’s a twist: LLMs are really good at producing average, "centroid" code. They interpolate from the wide swath of existing data, which is why so much AI-generated output leans toward safe, bland patterns—especially for popular technologies like React.

5/18 The real opportunity? Moving beyond one-size-fits-all software. We’re poised for "situated software"—hyper-tailored micro-apps that fit individual needs perfectly, but may look unpolished or bizarre from the outside. LLMs can make this viable at scale.

6/18 Yet the challenge is real—connecting these micro-apps into something bigger. The current “Google Docs for everything” approach can’t scale. We need new substrates and systems for these micro-apps to interact and add up to more than the sum of their parts.

1/16 Software is an extension of human agency—it has no will of its own. When you run code, you convert a creator’s potential into kinetic energy. Sometimes that's you; sometimes it's millions using code written long ago.

2/16 LLMs are different. If you let them drive, they absorb human agency. The more you lean on them as oracles, the more your steering wheel slips.

3/16 Agency is a muscle—you have to exercise it. If software makes decisions for you, your agency can atrophy. Stay alert to who’s really in control.

4/16 Programming with classic code is pure power: it does what you say, not what you mean. But now, LLMs “think” and that makes steering their outcomes much harder.

5/16 Feedback loops are everything. The longer it takes to see the outcome of an action, the harder it gets to learn and correct. Oversteer, oscillation, and delays become real problems in leadership and in AI systems.

6/16 In tech, seniority stretches those loops—your choices take years to reflect. By the time you see the effects, you're already off track. It’s a humbling twist: you are often your own saboteur, just on a lag.

7/16 LLMs and automations are only valuable if they’re reliable and fade into the background. But when they fail, it’s an emergency, and you’re rarely prepped to react. It’s the paradox of invisible infrastructure.

8/16 The stuff that makes LLMs possible: language → books → internet → LLMs. Each link in the chain is irreplaceable—no modern AI without the run-up of human knowledge.

9/16 Desktop OSes were data-centric; mobile OSes are app-centric. We need to return to a world where *your* data is at the center—portable, remixable, and truly yours.

12/16 Small talk is boring—big talk is illuminating. People rise to the level of curiosity you bring. Every conversation is a chance for discovery if you’re interested enough to actually listen.

1/16 LLMs don't just generate code—they churn out "glop": glue code that's functional, often unreadable, and destined to patch integrations until they work. It's not pretty, but soon, most code in the world will be glop. Embrace the black box! 

2/16 LLMs are compilers for English to code. Imagine sketching in half-English, half-code; LLMs can compile it down to executable, plain code. Only use the LLM at author-time, not runtime, for cost and speed.

3/16 Context is king. The real art with LLMs is curating tight, relevant context. Prune away tangents and dead ends to give LLMs exactly what they need—think of it as "gardening context." Products should make this context gardening natural and productive.

1/20 Software wants to be useful—just like genes "want" to replicate. If it isn’t useful, it won’t survive. Bitrot happens to apps no one runs anymore—selection pressure is *relentless*.

3/20 As user bases grow, products must cater to the lowest common denominator. The “tyranny of the marginal user” leads to bland, generic experiences.

4/20 AI is reducing the cost to make software. The boundaries between "app" and "not-an-app" will shift—why not have software that’s just for you, built on the fly?

5/20 MidJourney generates millions of images just for the creator—experiences with an audience of one. As creative costs plummet, why bother with mass distribution at all?

6/20 Imagine software so cheap to make, we get personalized, disposable micro-apps tailored only to us, in the moment. Software for the “market of one.”

7/20 Separating UX from data: UX is simple to copy, but data is expensive to move and requires trust. The key is doing interesting things *with your data*—the micro-app moment is about harnessing your data, safely and usefully.

8/20 Big AI labs excel at model-building, with powerful consumer demos almost by accident. Their edge isn't in UI—it’s the models. But that leaves room for others to build radically better user experiences or ecosystems on top.

9/20 Simple UX “sugar” can drive massive value by reducing friction and uncertainty. Just look at how Anthropic Artifacts 10x’d experimentation for non-programmer friends.

10/20 LLMs give non-coders intuitive control of code logic—the "DNA of code"—enabling hands-on tweaks and experimentation, even without classic programming skills.

11/20 Search engines and LLMs have strong data network effects from proprietary user behavior (querystream/clickstream). The real moat is in user data, not what’s publicly crawlable.

12/20 Proto-aggregators mimic giants but haven’t achieved true aggregation power yet. Many think they’re unstoppable—truth is, there are still many ways to outmaneuver them.

13/20 Aggregators inevitably optimize for engagement—even at the cost of user well-being or innovation. The “gravity well” of engagement optimization is nearly impossible to escape.

14/20 An open aggregator—a permissionless, trustless fabric—could combine the best of silos *and* centralized network effects, if we could make sharing data across origins truly safe.

15/20 What if we built Greasemonkey-style tinkering that’s actually safe? Unleash creativity *and* security.

16/20 LLMs as patient helpers may undermine traditional apprenticeship and tacit-knowledge transfer. Like calculators and math education—social adaptation could lag tech change.

17/20 Trust defines power concentration: products needing max trust will end up controlled by a few enormous entities. Edge cases have to be *amazing* to break in.

18/20 Narratives are powerful dimensionality reducers. They make a messy world manageable—but always distort reality. Don’t grip them too tightly. Be ready to update your frame.

19/20 Organizations grow kayfabe (performance, not reality) to the limit set by business/capital. Kayfabe is like gravity—it grows until it can’t, then chaos.

20/20 The things we all say and believe—aligned, even if individually minor—shape our collective reality. The Ouija board effect is real: what we expect can *become* what is.

1/ AI + LLMs aren't just new tools, they're a shift in the "gravity" of tech. Most playbooks from the last era don't work—LLMs are magical duct tape, not perfect machines. We have to unlearn old assumptions and embrace new forms of value creation.

1/ Modern software should be personal and aligned to *you*, not just the interests of a corporation. Agentic LLM-powered software is squishier—open-ended, unpredictable, potentially dangerous. Be cautious about what capabilities you grant it! 🧵

2/ The magic of AI agents isn’t just doing what they’re told—it’s looping on tool use between moments when you check in. They’re patient beyond human limits, able to comb through endless material or details without complaint. But that comes with risk and unpredictability.

3/ Data wants to aggregate, but why let it pool with for-profit megacorps? There’s power in open, personal aggregation. Let the gravity of your data work for you—not against you.

4/ Don’t just use LLMs to write for you—use them as thinking partners. Their outputs might be “frog DNA” mush if left on their own, but they have *good bounce* for idea development. Great convo partners make your ideas sharper by engaging, bouncing, riffing.

5/ Imagine building software like you craft in Minecraft: drop an app icon onto your data like a “magic sticker” and suddenly the sticker is alive, acting on your behalf. Mash up icons, make new custom apps—creation as play, not drudgery.

6/ LLM software by itself is mushy—generic, inhuman. But if you can retrieve exactly the past solutions others made for your problem, you tap into the collective consciousness—1000x more useful than a one-off, contextless answer.

7/ The vertical SaaS playbook crushes because wiring together arbitrary software is just too fiddly at scale. One-size-fits-all for a vertical = super-linear returns and sticky margin, especially where customer businesses are basically the same structurally.

8/ Decentralization and other ideals are great, but the primary use case must add *novel value*—most people trade purity for convenience. Ideals work best as bonuses, not main selling points.

9/ Don’t compare sketchy ideas to polished solutions. The longer you increase solution fidelity, the more mess you uncover. Compare the highest-fidelity existing options to avoid mirages—or raccoons with dissolving cotton candy dreams.

10/ The world is only getting weirder; trust in institutions is dropping, headlines get stranger, and LLMs accelerate that “weirdening.” Buckle up—it’s going to be a wild ride. 🌪️

1/22 A favorite analogy: ofuda. In Shinto, phrases on paper bring good fortune; in anime, they're spells. Programming is similar—write the right words, and you enchant computers. LLMs have made the magic accessible: now you can cast spells in plain English.

2/22 Software's cost equation has flipped. It used to be expensive to write, cheap to run. With LLMs, writing's cheap (especially for mini, disposable tools), but running LLM-powered code is expensive. The industry's foundational assumptions are upended!

3/22 Ideal pattern: use an LLM to write/configure code, but don’t ship the LLM inside the code. Only invoke magic when the software needs to *change*—not just to *run*. Cheaper, more sustainable!

4/22 Software has become disposable. I can whip out a 250-line webapp, and if it’s not perfect, who cares? Engineers don’t need to care about the guts for throwaway stuff. LLMs are perfect for this, especially for UIs you can directly test.

5/22 LLM-written software is mostly "good enough"—for small, similar problems. For more novel or complex creations? Less likely to hit the mark. But when humans tweak and approve, that work ratchets quality up for everyone in the future.

6/22 Imagine software where each use case can just use the data it needs—no friction, no giant cold start. Each working use case makes the system more valuable and shrinks the cold start for the next one. The ecosystem effect is *wildly* powerful.

7/22 We need new “laws of physics” for software. Contextual Flow Control = type-checking privacy policies. If it compiles, it respects user privacy by design. LLMs can do auto-iteration, resolving errors until the app is both correct AND privacy-aligned.

8/22 The cloud vs. local tension is real: local gives agency, privacy, control, but the cloud brings scale & convenience. Private cloud enclaves promise both data sovereignty *and* the benefits of the cloud. This could change who can safely benefit from cloud power.

9/22 Users don’t need to understand security plumbing; they just need to trust someone who does, all the way to experts. Trust diffuses—people will come to rely on new architectures if a trusted node does first.

10/22 If the software ecosystem is open, anyone can plug in new use cases—reducing the cold start problem for niche features and pooling value. This collective snowball effect is a moat: new rivals can’t match the systemic data momentum.

11/22 LLMs let everyone be mediocre at a wider range of tasks. If you’re world-class, your excellence stands out more. But it might erode deep craftsmanship since learning happens in the making, not just the result. Are we sacrificing mastery for speed?

12/22 Not everything needs to be self-driving—sometimes electrifying the tools we already know (like e-bikes for the mind) unlocks new capability with familiar affordances. The adjacent possible is more valuable than a distant moonshot.

13/22 Our competence is limited by what we *don’t realize* we don’t know. The four stages of competence model reminds me: we’re all missing blindspots. LLMs help, but can also reinforce superficial understanding.

14/22 Challenge powers growth. When you’re very powerful, you lose challenge (and learning), because relationships get sycophantic. Regular, honest pushback is essential—hence, recurring 1:1s matter more than we realize.

15/22 Avoid premature convergence in ambiguity. It feels safer to lock in decisions early, but staying in superposition keeps you adaptable. The cost? Stress. The upside? Resilience and better calibration.

16/22 Progress = disconfirming evidence. But it stings. The trick: create psychological safety so feedback is affordable, not threatening—crucial if you want innovation, not stagnation.

17/22 Stories evolve into caricatures. Each time they're told, memories morph, compressing to fit a tidy narrative. We remember the gist, not the details; memory is dynamic, not archival.

18/22 Centralization mimics a healthy ecosystem but kills true resilience. One bad corporate decision can wipe out all our "third places"—local flavor and antifragility lost in a snap.

19/22 Fascinating: in complex systems, selection can happen by changing which particles are in the swarm, not just by changing the particles. Adaptation doesn’t require intent—emergence works through biased attrition.

20/22 How long do you let people prototype divergently before reining them in? It depends on their track record for discovery *and* their ability to help everyone converge. Letting explorers run ahead (with a leash!) can be a massive force-multiplier.

21/22 A big innovation pattern: take robust, manual processes and turn them into general, automated ones. The investment turns bespoke hacks into scalable infrastructure, freeing up attention for even more innovation.

22/22 Most important: find *intriguing* challenges—ones with the promise of discovery and progress as you go. That’s what keeps motivation (and the flywheel of learning) spinning.

1/🧵 I see platform development as a swarm of uncoordinated energy—be it devs, users, or contributors. The key to coherence isn’t rigid control, but creating selection pressure and North Stars that gently bias the swarm to converge. #platforms #complexsystems

2/ Instead of a "builder" mindset obsessed with control, I advocate for a "gardener" mindset: influence the ecosystem with scaffolding, not by micromanaging every entity. There’s far more power in strategic nudges than in illusory control. #leadership

3/ The challenge in open, complex systems is not to force alignment from day one—instead, sketch a vision, take small coherent actions, and let self-selecting collaborators join in. Forced consensus kills momentum; self-selection feeds it. #platformstrategy

4/ Coherence isn’t about eliminating diversity; it’s about making sure innovation isn’t lost as noise. Protocols and shared APIs—be they intentional or emergent—give tinkerers boundaries to build meaningfully, not just randomly. #openculture

5/ I’m bullish about generative AI: it’s unlocking a new era for tinkerers, reducing transaction costs and making it way easier to experiment and compose solutions. Let’s swing back from too much selection pressure and unleash more creative energy. #generativeAI

6/ Trust is foundational. Hire great people, give them freedom, and resist the urge to control every detail. Kayfabe—playing along with organizational myths—can be dangerous when it disconnects from reality. Stay grounded, not just performative. #orgdesign

8/ Ultimately: design for participation, think long term, and own the implications of what you build. The true moral compass? Would I be proud to watch a replay of today’s decisions, 10 years from now, with friends and family? That’s my north star. #ethics #leadership

1/ I think of LLMs as "imitation intelligence"—they don’t reason, they mimic. Like imitation meat, they almost get it right, but it’s a bit off. They follow vibes and patterns, not deep understanding. Meet them at the surface, not at first principles.

3/ The biggest fear around LLMs isn’t just about data leaving our control—it’s the invisible threat: your data could be absorbed and repurposed in ways you may never see, reappearing years later in unexpected forms. The unknown risk is more chilling than the known.

4/ Confidential compute that locks out the cloud host is only half the puzzle. What really matters is locking out the *service provider*—the one who truly handles your data and could mishandle it. We need private cloud enclaves—confidential from all, verifiable by you.

5/ Hallucinated LLM mini-apps are hot right now—like WebSim, Windows9x—but the big blockers: no safe data handling, and no way to compose them together. The next leap? Privately secure bases for composable, persistent, and safe LLM-driven applications.

6/ LLMs operate on superficial similarity, not true fundamentals. If your problem doesn’t look like something in their dataset, they get lost. Curated examples outperform fancy prompts because they capture the vibe you want—even the stuff you forgot to specify.

7/ It’s wild: LLMs flood us with generated noise, but they’re also great at summarizing and decluttering. The email arms race—LLMs generating wordy prose, other LLMs reducing it back to the outline.
