8/16 Luxury brands aren't just about intrinsic quality—they trade on the signaling value. When the signaling dominates and the core product falters, both values erode—sometimes explosively. Beware the feedback loop!

9/16 A useful narrative organizes reality. But the more self-coherent the story, the more I risk ignoring inconvenient facts. Narratives can convince—but once counter-evidence emerges, they can shatter fast.

10/16 Two core mindsets for self-bootstrapping: 1) See the world’s shades of gray, not just black and white. 2) Lean IN to the unexpected with curiosity. These compound into wisdom—tools for future problems.

11/16 Intractable problems can crush us, unless we treat them as intellectual puzzles, not moral failings. Letting go of "this should be different" lets me play with the systems as they are—and sometimes, find fresh judo moves.

12/16 When stuck in checkmate, look at the containing system! Every system sits within another. Moves from the next level can upend the game—sometimes feeling like "cheating," but often the real escape.

13/16 Insights are seeds you lob to others. But if the receiver isn’t open, they’ll feel like pebbles. Seeds can lay dormant, waiting for the right soil and season to bloom.

14/16 Tip: mentor others. Helping distill your insights helps you clarify your own thinking. If you capture these seeds in a knowledge management system, you’re mentoring yourself, compounding insight over time.

15/16 Forces like gravity—mundane but omnipresent—are often invisible until they change. Question the background forces you take for granted; their shift can create unthinkable ripple effects.

16/16 Always allow counterparties (users, peers) to self-select. Intrinsic motivation drives real alignment. Spread your butterfly net wide—cultivate, don’t coerce. That’s how you nurture a thriving system.

1/🧵 We often mistake an empty "blue ocean" for opportunity, but if a space is well-known and still empty, it’s likely dead—not blue. Hidden constraints mean success there isn't just unlikely, it's impossible. Beware the urge to underestimate absent counter-examples. (1/9)

2/ Quality isn't about cost—it's about a ceiling. The Economist & USA Today may spend similarly per word, but Economist-level quality can’t scale. Beyond a certain point, the price of expanding quality becomes *effectively infinite.* Scale is its own constraint. (2/9)

3/ Clean, beautiful products are less sticky than messy, customizable ones. Customers want to tinker—that stickiness is what keeps them. The mess creates nooks for engagement and investment. Don’t fear complexity; leverage it to build loyalty. (3/9)

4/ Organizational strength comes from dynamic tension—like tensegrity structures. Flexibility to shift balance is vital. Too much fixed structure ossifies adaptation: what was once alive becomes static and dead. Keep your organizational muscles limber. (4/9)

5/ One-ply thinking is a trap. Strategic thinking means going beyond first-order effects—multi-ply, multi-step reasoning gives the edge. Just thinking one level deeper than everyone else isn't enough; true strategy means seeing 10 moves ahead. (5/9)

6/ Platforms and aggregators look similar, but key difference: platforms create possibility without a ceiling; aggregators set the ceiling. Aggregators lift from the top, but ultimately contain you. True platforms keep things open-ended. (6/9)

7/ Protocols and tacit knowledge: Explicit rules aren’t enough. Most protocols (APIs, contracts, even laws) quietly rely on shared, non-written knowledge. Sometimes you can expose this tacit layer, but sometimes making it explicit destroys the magic. (7/9)

8/ Sometimes more *limitation* unlocks more value. Sandboxes and the web’s security model made exploring safer, spawning more creativity. Restriction reduces downside, inviting more players. Apple's App Store sandbox: powerful, but also a hard ceiling. (8/9)

9/ LLMs, like brains, coordinate fragmented steps to create an illusion of coherence— each “token” is a new agent stitching meaning from memory. We’re all a bit like slime molds: emergent intelligence from many parts, not a single mastermind. (9/9)

1/ AI isn’t meant to replace us or trap us with endless engagement. I dream of "Intentional Tech"—technology aligning perfectly with MY intentions, not a corporation’s. The era of AI demands tools that extend my agency, not hijack my attention.

2/ Most current tech is built for engagement, not my higher goals. Doomscrolling? No one INTENDS to do it. We need technology that supports what we aspire to, not what we compulsively fall into.

3/ LLMs are a new kind of computer—stochastic, creative, but unpredictable. Like humans, they have both mechanistic (system 1) and emergent (system 2) modes. The future isn’t about choosing one—it’s blending both for max benefit.

4/ Chatbots are just the obvious, first-order use of LLMs. Great for starting things, but useless for long-term, structured orchestration. We’re missing the wild potential for AI-native, coactive software that self-assembles around our real needs.

5/ Your context is a goldmine. Aggregators are scrambling to lock it up. But context should belong to YOU—not be “fracked” for profit. Imagine a private, personalized intelligence: totally aligned with your interests, invisible to outsiders.

6/ Power without alignment is terrifying. Proactive AI feels scary when it’s not truly personal. I want a system that works only for me—my exocortex—to help me think, organize, and flourish WITHOUT surveillance or surrendering my agency.

7/ The problem isn’t context or personalization—it’s misaligned incentives. Personalized AI doesn’t have to mean surveillance. What if you could pay for a service where even the company CAN’T see or access your data?

8/ Most people don’t want “a better Notion.” We want a librarian. AI tools that structure, curate, and compound our own information in ways that feel like magic—no more crawling through broken glass to keep life organized.

9/ We don’t have to accept the current app-centric, aggregator-dominated world. Decentralized, user-owned “coactive fabrics" can unlock a human-centric, flourishing future for AI. It’s time to step out of the engagement gravity well.

1/ Thread: APIs vs. Platforms 🧵

1. There's a real difference between an API and a platform. An API has well-defined boundaries—if you use it, you know exactly where it starts and ends. (1/n)

2. A platform is messier. Its boundaries blur as it absorbs the chaos of real-world use cases. Platforms thrive because the world *is* messy. If a platform can't be bent to odd needs, it won't see broad adoption. (2/n)

3. When building, you optimize APIs for focus and simplicity. You optimize platforms to "never say no"—to allow almost anything, even if it's not always easy. (3/n)

4. Platforms are sticky: every incremental use-case a customer invests in makes them less likely to leave. Value = empowerment to solve problems not otherwise solvable. (4/n)

5. Spreadsheets are the OG "platform for calculation." Their messiness IS the feature—they allow chaotic MVPs to exist and lower the bar to try new ideas. They'll never go away. (5/n)

6. Sometimes, once a use case is common enough, it graduates out of spreadsheets into bespoke tools. But the flexibility of a platform is the bedrock for all sorts of creation. (6/n)

7. TL;DR: If you want wide adoption, let your platform get messy. Embrace mess as the texture of opportunity. (7/7)

2/ Thread: Why Patience—and Shared Challenge—Matters

1. Patience is my secret weapon. Apple's best products? They're baked with patience—you can't rush quality, just like you can't bake a cake in 60% of the time. (1/n)

2. Intrinsic motivation grows things stronger. You can't force a seedling; you have to nurture it. Same goes for teams and products. (2/n)

3. Scaling too soon turns order into chaos—laminar to turbulent flow. If you add too much energy, you just get more chaos. Patience is how you build durability. (3/n)

4. Shared challenge bonds teams. Early startups live on the thrill of make-or-break work; as you grow, it's easy to slip into burnout mode if you fight only for optics, not real outcomes. (4/n)

5. Nuance takes time to absorb—shades of gray, not just black and white. In a noisy world, speed pushes us toward sharper, but less true, beliefs. (5/n)

6. TL;DR: Don't rush growth or feedback. Invest in patience, nurture the creative core, and value bonds built through real shared challenge. (6/6)

1/ LLMs are the radically lower-cost input for the new tech paradigm—cheaper than human mental labor. They appear like "reasoners," but what's really going on is world-class pattern matching—absurdly good intuition masquerading as logic. (1/n)

2/ Most AI use cases are too inflexible or expensive to package as standalone apps—and too bland without deep context. The magic of LLMs lies in their adaptability to complex, context-rich environments, not as fixed app products. (2/n)

3/ Our digital universe is shaped by the "same origin model": every web/app domain is its own tiny pocket universe, walled off and starting data-empty. This model enabled safe web experiences—but created powerful gravity wells for centralization. (3/n)

4/ As origins amass data, network effects magnify: the big get bigger. This leads to centralization, confusing permissions, and a world of apps that feel like huge Duplo blocks—clunky to combine, granting little user agency. (4/n)

5/ I've become a fan of "local first" software, but that's only part of the puzzle. The real win is reclaiming control over data not just from third parties, but from the cages of the apps/origins themselves—so users can create new value by combining their own data. (5/n)

6/ Malleable software is built from small, rigid building blocks—as tiny as grains of sand—that users can mix and match. The smaller the blocks, the more fluid and powerful the system can be in the user's hands. (6/n)

7/ LLMs are society-scale crystallized intuition. For enforcement, they provide "good-enough" default policies where 99% agree—letting us handle ambiguity the way humans do, not with rigid mechanical rules, but with organic, adaptable judgment. (7/n)

8/ UI tip: the 4-up option (like MidJourney) is a killer paradigm—not just for images, but for any complex state navigation. Presenting multiple options massively lowers the cost of being "wrong" and lets users course-correct with little friction. (8/n)

9/ High-trust environments, reversible errors, and positive deviance are keys to innovation. Don’t just design for lazy users—design for creative ones who want to take initiative and tinker with tools. (9/n)

10/ The open ecosystem will always win vs. aggregators, all else equal. A small but distinct edge in freedom and lower downside risk compounds rapidly thanks to network effects; central gatekeepers are always one inciting incident away from collapse. (10/n)

11/ The way forward is rewilding the internet—shaking off brittle monocultures, rediscovering open-endedness, composability, and agency over our tools. That’s how we’ll escape digital feudalism and regain creative power. (11/11)

1/12 I used to think "heroics"—fire-fighting, adrenaline-pumping hard work—made me valuable. The praise, the rush, the sense of saving the day… I got addicted. But over time, I saw it was a destructive cycle. 🧵

2/12 Heroics are easy to celebrate, especially in tech. Who doesn’t love a hero? But here’s the secret: Heroics are the lowest leverage work you can do. You’re fixing what could’ve been avoided with a little foresight.

3/12 The more I dove into heroics, the more I judged others who weren’t constantly sprinting. I thought they were lazy. In truth, I was wrong—and closing myself off from their perspectives made everything worse.

4/12 The heroics spiral: past quick fixes become the roots of current crises. It feels like the only escape is more heroics, but it actually drags everyone down and increases the chaos.

5/12 In reality, my heroics created tiny local order, but wrecked wider team coherence. Others had to clean up invisible messes I left behind. The more heroics you use, the weaker your systems get.

6/12 Heroics are fine for tiny teams or urgent existential threats—but as organizations grow, relying on heroics is a disaster. It creates fragility and chaos that only gets worse over time.

7/12 Why do we keep falling for heroics? 1️⃣ They come from genuine care. 2️⃣ They’re essential in real crises. 3️⃣ They’re celebrated—so it’s hard to see (or admit) their negative effects.

8/12 As you move from 0→1 to 1→10, the need for heroics should drop. If it doesn’t, you’re likely laying a foundation of thrash and churn, not of real, durable growth.

9/12 If you’re a leader, your heroics’ negative ripple effects go even further. The higher up you go, the less likely anyone will point them out. Being celebrated doesn’t mean you’re helping.

10/12 The fix? Step back. Take a holistic view. Make tiny systemic tweaks that turn entropy into coherence. Slow, thoughtful growth makes teams strong. Endless fire-fighting just creates more fires.

11/12 It takes courage NOT to join the chaos. Carve out time for real synthesis and reflection. That’s where superlinear returns and real organizational value are created.

12/12 If you spot the addiction to heroics in yourself or others, call it out. We need less heroics, not more. The most valuable work is quiet, strategic, and lasting—not always loud and heroic.

1/ The era of "infinite software" is here: LLMs make creating software so cheap we're no longer limited by supply, but by demand. The challenge isn’t making apps, it's making *meaningful* software that works *for you*, not Big Tech. #BetterTech

2/ Apps are a dead-end metaphor for this new era. They're isolated islands, great only if there's one that matches your needs. But most of what you need is below the "Coasian Floor"—too personal or niche to be worth traditional development. #InfiniteSoftware

3/ The future: personalized, micro-apps that swarm together on your data, tailored exactly for you. But this requires a new, open platform—one that's safe, private, and lets untrusted code work with your sensitive data. The old security models aren't enough.

4/ Prompt injection is the new ceiling. It’s not just a security issue—it’s about PRIVACY. If AI can be tricked into exfiltrating your private data, then you don’t really own it. Solving this unlocks the true power of LLMs and safe integration.

5/ We need an open, privacy-preserving substrate where vibe-coded micro-apps can interact safely. Not an aggregator monopoly—something decentralized, with a new security model, where YOU control your data and your experiences. #MeaningfulComputing

6/ In this new world, the best tech will truly align with your aspirations and adapt to you—not just offer bland, one-size-fits-none solutions. It’s time to build technology that nourishes our souls, not just hooks our attention.

1/ 🧵 The most "lovable" software is often the most insecure—and not just technically. There's a rawness, a fragility, that feels alive but also risky. This is both a feature and a flaw. It’s the software equivalent of shouting secrets in the dark.

2/ Alan Watts' “Wisdom of Insecurity” tells us life is uncertain—seeking absolute safety kills the spirit. Vibe-coded tools, built by enthusiasts, are expressions of faith as much as belief. But that leap of faith online is a real risk. Can we preserve that intimacy without betraying trust?

3/ I love Clay Shirky's idea of "situated software": ugly, niche, perfect for its tiny use case. The challenge is building a substrate that allows this bottom-up authenticity but safely—so users don’t accidentally let their dreams leak into the wrong hands.

4/ Apps as we know them are too static and isolating—they’re little islands. What we need are networks for remixing and adding to one another’s creations, blurring the lines between creator and consumer. It’s about enabling creators, not just consumers.

5/ Trust is hardest when asymmetries are invisible. Big tech environments know us better than we know ourselves—without reciprocal trust. I'd rather risk the sketchy boat I can see than be manipulated by an ocean that masks its intentions. It’s about clarity in what we risk.

6/ Cozy punk and vibe-coded tools remind me of Mark Fisher's “hauntology”—software that echoes futures that never happened. These tools may feel warm and handmade, but do they challenge the roots of industrial software, or just offer nostalgia with new paint?

7/ We've gained abundance—tools, agency—but with a loss: intimacy. Agency without human connection leads to existential loneliness. It’s not that chatbots are too smart; it’s that the social fabric is thin enough to make them feel like friends.

8/ The right question isn’t “Is tech good or bad?” but “What wound is this tech trying to heal—and what new wounds might it open?” We need to build with an awareness of trade-offs, not just charge ahead assuming progress is always positive.

9/ We keep swinging between centralization (average, safe, frictionless) and decentralization (cozy, messy, real communities). The real opportunity lies in balancing agency and intimacy—letting us move from consumers to creators, but not at the cost of meaningful connection.

10/ Flourishing isn’t just human—it’s societal, planetary. We need epistemic humility: we don’t know everything, and we shouldn’t pretend to. Let’s build tech with imagination, reverence, and eyes wide open to what we win and what we lose. #tech #society #philosophy

1/ LLMs are powerful not just alone, but when paired with a high-taste human curator. Most LLM "creativity" is actually co-creation—the human prompts, selects, shapes, and the LLM provides open-ended raw material. It's irreducibly collaborative. (1/13)

2/ The current chat UI for LLMs is like the command line before GUIs. It lets us have open-ended, meandering conversations, but it feels constricting. I'm craving a way to prune and fork threads, explore alternate realities, and edit past steps—true creative workflows! (2/13)

3/ Interacting with LLMs is early days computing—specialists required, creative potential untapped. The real innovation will come from inventing the equivalent of GUIs for LLMs—a radical rethink of the interaction model, not just an iteration on chat. (3/13)

4/ LLMs unlock best-practices synthesis: hand them 3 templates (e.g. project management) and they instantly surface the common thread—the domain's essentials. Boring, tedious synthesis for us is effortless for them. (4/13)

5/ Unlike regular programs, LLMs are open-ended—capable of creativity, but inherently unpredictable. Instead of running them in your main loop, use them as on-demand enhancers for convergent, closed software—like a call center operator's manual with human judgment. (5/13)

6/ "Good enough" isn't a compromise with perfection; it's the toehold that makes an idea viable. Once it's possible and easy, we can iterate up to greatness. Most of the time, "great" isn't even on the table—just delivering something that works is a breakthrough. (6/13)

7/ LLMs are like electricity: a general-purpose technology that instantly changes what inputs are cheap and feasible. Now that "crappy little bits of software" are basically free, a whole new landscape of possibilities opens up. We're just at the beginning. (7/13)

8/ LLMs aren't "all of society"—they're a slice, with biases. Just like a Hollywood blockbuster, they reflect dominant cultures. As LLMs become more central, whose perspective they amplify will become a critical social issue. (8/13)

9/ The rise of LLMs is like ecological adaptive radiation after a mass extinction: a disruptive new force changes the cost structure, opens niches, and unleashes evolutionary chaos. Specialists may go extinct, but generalists thrive as new "species" appear. (9/13)

10/ A/B testing (without vision) can trap you in infinitely recursive local maxima. True innovation demands a bigger leap—an intentional jolt, not just the path of least resistance. (10/13)

11/ Modern life is dominated by supernormal stimuli, powered by runaway capitalism and culture. Tech and commerce have gotten great at giving us what our lizard brains crave, not what we aspire to want. The results: junk food, information overload, and more. (11/13)

12/ Greatness in teams comes from three ingredients: abrasion (surface disconfirming evidence), agility (navigate, simplify, adapt), and resolution (distill and improve together). It's not just about being nice—it's about surfacing and processing surprises. (12/13)

13/ Play leads to the best work. Structure some unstructured time! Through beginner's mind and playful exploration, rather than only chasing external goals, we unlock true creativity. (13/13)

1/🧵 LLMs shift knowledge from world-aligned (allocentric) to user-aligned (egocentric). Books/media were made for everyone—now, ideas rotate around *you*: your context, needs, biases. Powerful, but can deepen echo chambers and extremity.

2/ One unrealized superpower of LLMs: infinite patience. So many stop asking questions out of embarrassment—LLMs never judge, never tire. They could break open self-doubt as a learning barrier.

3/ LLMs are set to supercharge legalese. Non-lawyers can now summon or interpret dense contracts, raising the complexity equilibrium for everyone. It's like adding lanes to highways: no relief, just more traffic (but legal docs).

4/ Chat feels amorphous—fun for brainstorming, but terrible for repeatable, actionable info. We lost physical affordances (like buttons) with touchscreens. In chatbots, it's just undifferentiated text. Is this the UI future?

5/ Don’t trust LLMs unconditionally—they’re too eager to please. Always slip in a ‘reverse Van Halen brown M&M’ check: ask for something obviously wrong to see if it pushes back, or just agrees.

6/ The AI arms race is real. Everyone knows AI is "the thing"—so every org slaps on whatever AI bandwagon (like MCP), even if it’s a dead-end. Implementation talk > real use. The shiny toy may be a mirage.

7/ LLMs are best used to *write* software, not be the software. Let them generate code on demand, which is concrete & verifiable, vs relying on their squishy, gullible reasoning skills for critical tasks. Code is trustable; chat isn’t.

1/🧵 Agency explodes when tools are malleable, shareable, distributed, composable, interoperable, and computational. But perfectly bespoke tools rot away unless they self-maintain—otherwise, you'll return to find your unique widget dead.

2/ Current security means trusting app makers with your data. What if code goes to your data (not vice versa)? Then trust is baked in—creators *can’t* peek. Paranoid security means users can relax.

3/ Claude Code is safe—version control means no scary irreversible mistakes. But general autonomous agents? Yikes. One bad instruction, and your core systems can get wrecked, no takebacks.

4/ If apps weld data to themselves, users are stuck. ATProto & BlueSky flip this, letting you swap algorithms/apps while keeping your data—like owning your digital house, not couchsurfing on Gmail.

5/ Aggregators control user territory. True open ecosystems must design for future decentralization—not consensus-by-averaging (which makes mush) but federations of wild ideas, some of which will prove viable & break out.

6/ LLMs could bring back small tech businesses: if human-level ranking is cheap, we don’t need massive userbases to add real value. A throwback to the 80s/90s—but powered by modern AI.

7/ Society gets ever more pancake-like—broad & shallow for speed and interoperability, but less depth, meaning, and community. Are we optimizing out nuance for efficiency’s sake?

8/ With great tech power comes massive responsibility. Abdicating the indirect effects—chasing efficiency at all costs—hollows everything out. It’s time to imagine software (and tech) as it *should* be, not just as it is.

1/ Unlocking miracles in product strategy isn’t about massive bets—it’s about cultivating serendipity. I call this the "doorbell in the jungle" pattern: place cheap, low-risk options that expose you to big, unexpected upside. 🛎️🌴

2/ Most orgs obsess over the "Serious Business" approach: only invest in ideas with a huge forecasted return. But what if you could take tiny bets with capped downside and uncapped upside? It’s less risky, more creative—and often game-changing.

3/ Miracles rarely arrive on schedule. By putting up lots of little “doorbells” (tiny features, sign-up forms, hidden options), you create optionality. Respond only if real users ring the bell with motivated demand.

4/ Stop building massive gates without knowing who wants to walk through them. Let users hack through the brambles first—early adopters prove their hunger. Then pave the way once it’s clear they’re coming back for more.

5/ This isn’t vaporware—these “doorbells” are cheap to set up, visible enough for the desperate or curious, and easy to scale if real demand materializes. Every incremental move is justified by reality, not speculation.

6/ PMF isn’t only about huge markets. A tiny, motivated group + low setup costs = instant product-market fit for *them*. Expand gradually by removing friction for the next wave of slightly less-motivated users.

7/ Real-world examples: limited developer sign-ups, informal template sharing, geek modes, multi-bucket rankings—each lets motivated users reveal new growth paths, often invisible from the C-suite.

8/ The real secret? Maximize your “miracle surface area.” Don’t fight to prove hypothetical upside. Minimize and cap your downside, patiently react to real signals, and let your portfolio of doorbells cultivate luck itself.

9/ It’s tempting to look for certainty and control, but greatness often comes from letting go—trusting that if you create enough tiny openings, you’ll stumble on something miraculous and real.

1/13 When I look at an old, messy system, I try to see not just what's broken, but what it could become. The fact that it’s heavily used means it has leverage—and that’s a privilege. Small, humble improvements can make it soar.

2/13 If you’re sharing metrics externally, beware: every precedent you set becomes a burden. People assume silence means bad news. Share at random, unpredictable intervals to avoid setting expectations you’ll regret.

3/13 At dev-focused companies, is the API or the dashboard UX more important? The API is essential for power users, but great UX expands who can use your product. The best is when you don’t have to touch the API unless you want to.

4/13 Built things (like code or buildings) and living things (like humans or trees) are radically different. Built things require a top-down design; living things adapt locally, growing stronger where there’s stress, no big plan required.

5/13 UX changes are more forgiving—they’re for people, and people adapt. API changes are for code, and code is brittle. Most code is executed far more than it’s thought about. That’s where code gets its leverage (and risk).

6/13 You only really understand a system when you can predict its behavior. Black boxes—where you can’t see state—make this much harder. More signals = better intuition. Fewer signals = lost users.

7/13 Deep quality isn’t free. If you won’t make tough trade-offs (invest in QA, refactor relentlessly), you’re only pretending to value quality.

8/13 Never execute a multi-step plan where step 1 alone isn’t valuable. Much better to pursue actions that are worthwhile even if you stop after the first step.

9/13 Everything diffuses by default—entropy. Innovation thrives in this chaos, but durable things require focus and coordination. Breadth brings diversity; depth brings coherence. Both have their costs.

10/13 Agency is collapsing the wave of possibilities into the thing you actually do. Past choices narrow the path ahead, but context and others’ decisions constrain what’s possible.

11/13 If you know you get more than one shot, you’ll take bigger risks. That’s how cooperation (like in the prisoner’s dilemma) can emerge—when there’s a next round.

12/13 The magic happens at boundaries—where discontinuities exist. New things emerge when you blend ideas from both sides of a divide.

13/13 Long-term thinking (and democracy) takes practice. If your world is chaotic, or you’re always moving, it gets harder to think long-term. Stability helps you plan for the future.

1/🧵 Ever noticed how every "one-size-fits-none" app frustrates users by missing your one must-have feature? It's not laziness—it's complexity. Every new feature adds clutter for everyone else, not just you. 

2/ The more powerful software gets, the more each user ends up with bloat they don't need. Complexity = everything you *don't* use, but can't escape.

3/ But now, with LLMs, writing "software in the small" is affordable. Imagine software that's personal by default—fits like a glove. Mass-personalization is now possible.

4/ Want real control? Jailbreak your data. Get it out of the cage of standardized apps. Tinker and command on your turf.

5/ LLMs aren't deeply wise—they just absorb patterns from examples, like "baked shadows" in old video games. Looks real, but it's all surface. Flood them with examples, and their recognition is robust—but don't mistake it for depth.

6/ Humans see agency in anything with a face. A Tamagotchi, an LLM chat UI—same trick. LLMs are pond scum intelligence wearing a friendly mask. We trust the "face," but they're not truly smart.

7/ Modern tools like ValTown? Not "no code," just "convenient code." When you work in the small, architecture falls away, and you flex creativity. Constraints give surprising power—what if you could run these on your *most sensitive* data?

8/ What if your software worked like the elves from The Elves and the Shoemaker—quietly fulfilling your wishes at night? Proactive, personal, and (crucially) only acting in *your* interest, not some creepily general agenda.

9/ TL;DR: The future isn't massive apps stuffed with every feature. It's highly personal, small bits of code, enabled by LLMs, running on your data for *you*. Unlock the box, free your data, claim software that fits *you*.

1/15 Let's talk about the chaos of modern web development: too many incompatible widgets, endless JavaScript spaghetti, and DOM cruft everywhere. It's not your fault—the platform itself needs an upgrade! #WebComponents

2/15 Widgets today are a mess. Want to use a tab strip from one lib and a date picker from another? Too bad! They don't interop. That's why we end up with messy iframes, duplicated functionality, and headaches. #WebDev

3/15 My nightmare? Inspecting Gmail's DOM. All that nested, inexplicable markup. But that's what it takes to make things work, because the base web platform is missing core pieces. #Frontend

4/15 We're patching every pothole in the web highway with yet more JS. One detour leads to another, until we're way off-road, trapped in bizarro JavaScript land. #WebPlatform

5/15 Frameworks try to help, but they're forced to build their own abstractions since native browser APIs don't offer the primitives we really need. More code, more bugs, more misery. #CodeQuality

6/15 But here's the good news: Web Components are fixing this. They fill those missing gaps—in the platform itself—so we can finally build, share, and use components natively. #WebComponents

7/15 Web Components = Shadow DOM (encapsulation), Custom Elements (my own tags!), Templates (reusable HTML). Together, these tools let us build real widgets right in the browser, with cleaner code and saner HTML. #Innovation

8/15 Imagine inspecting your app's DOM and seeing just the semantic pieces you intend—no more DOM horror. All the presentational cruft is hidden, just like video controls are native but not in the markup. #ShadowDOM

9/15 Shadow DOM lets components keep their internals private. Styles and markup are shielded, so your stuff can't get stomped by other people's CSS—or vice versa. No more etiquette, just real guardrails. #CSS

10/15 Custom Elements mean I can define my own HTML tags, complete with APIs and behaviors, that just work. No more juggling parallel object hierarchies in JS and DOM.

11/15 Templates let me author chunks of HTML that don't do anything until I want to stamp them out, with real HTML syntax—not string soup. Reusability, encapsulation, readability. #WebDev

12/15 The result? We finally get interoperable components, from any library or author. They can be mixed, matched, improved, and shared. No more "tabstrip for jQuery, Dojo, Prototype..."—one tabstrip to rule them all. #OpenWeb

14/15 This is the path to a declarative renaissance: less code, faster render, more native speed. No build steps, no hacks. And browser vendors are on board—this is a true community effort. #HTML5 #WebStandards

3/n

1/ Probably the biggest shifts in tech & orgs come from rethinking the underlying “laws of physics”—the Copernican Shift. Most complexity in legacy systems? Just epicycles glued on top of an old model. Swap models, and suddenly everything gets simpler.

2/ So much of today’s software complexity is just us patching over models that don’t fit how people *actually* want to use services. We need more structural shifts—less duct tape, more transformation.

3/ LLMs are wild: they’re brilliant at echoing the world’s “vibes” but stumble hard on seemingly simple tasks, like big JSON blobs or handling “negative space.” They aren’t computing, they’re matching patterns from their massive memory.

4/ Want LLMs to improve? Make feedback immediate: tests everywhere! The more automated feedback loops (tests) you layer in, the better these autopilot systems can correct themselves. LLMs make tests easier to write too.

5/ Looped AI is underrated—having two or more AIs critique and iterate together can yield way smarter outcomes than just one. It’s not the individual AI that’s smart; it’s the feedback loop that’s the magic.

1/ Meaning takes time; optimization is fast. Most of our systems turbocharge optimization (tight feedback loops, monocultures, overfitting to the niche), but struggle to make anything *meaningful* or adaptable.

2/ A living organization is able to actually respond to surprises, not just collapse under them. Bureaucracies often drain away alive responsibility, replacing it with dead process—missing critical issues everyone “saw” but no one owned.

3/ The best ranking systems aren’t just clever algorithms—they harness the collective intelligence of millions. The “swarm’s” behavior, aggregated, is where superlinear gains come from. Don’t cut out the humans; they’re your best truth signal.

4/ Feedback loops shape everything: the tighter the loop, the more you risk monocultures and catastrophic failure if your assumptions turn out wrong. Flexible building blocks—remixable, robust—make you more alive and adaptable to surprise.

5/ In orgs and platforms, beware the aggregator’s “peek and poach”: let 3P ecosystems experiment, then quickly hoist the best ideas into the 1P layer, starving the ecosystem. This dynamic is everywhere—especially in today’s AI-powered chat UIs.

1/12 Technology on the web started with desktops: powerful machines, constant power, and always-on connections. The magic was low friction—you clicked a link, and you were in. We take that for granted, but it's *still* critical for businesses.

2/12 Then mobile arrived. At first, everyone thought web apps would rule on phones too, but the world went app-first instead. Why? Because native apps offer richer experiences—but only for your most dedicated users willing to make the big commitment to install.

3/12 The app funnel is super leaky: every step from site visit ➡️ app banner ➡️ install means losing more users. Most people never make it through—often only low single digits make it to the end. That means tons of potential value gets lost.

4/12 Not everyone wants to install an app for something they do rarely. If you sell couches, people aren’t buying one every week! For most, apps are too big a commitment.

5/12 The web never went away on mobile but, honestly, many mobile web experiences have been pretty lackluster—because we just shrank our desktop websites instead of truly rethinking what mobile users need in the moment.

6/12 Here’s the real magic: mobile users are often in 'found time'—waiting for coffee, killing time in a cab. They want inspiration, quick engagement, not necessarily a big purchase right away. We need to meet them where they are, in *that* context.

7/12 This isn't something only apps can do! By intentionally designing for mobile web, we’ve seen much more engaging, immersive experiences—if we actually invest, instead of just porting.

8/12 But: classic mobile web had major gaps. Three big ones: 1️⃣ Connectivity—mobile is unreliable; 2️⃣ Push notifications—users expect them; 3️⃣ Typing—nobody likes it on mobile!

9/12 Through industry-wide standards work, we’ve solved these! That’s what powers Progressive Web Apps (PWAs): Service workers, add-to-homescreen, web push. If a browser supports them, the user gets a better, more app-like experience—without the app commitment.

10/12 This means smoother, less "do-or-die" engagement. Someone interested in a couch but not ready to buy? Just let them get notified when it’s in stock—no need for a full app install. It's a paradigm shift: you can now build lasting relationships on the web.

11/12 PWAs are a global phenomenon. Major brands—Forbes, Washington Post, Twitter, Lyft, and big e-commerce players—are seeing huge lifts in engagement and conversion. The key is thinking mobile-first and investing in the right experience for the moment.

12/12 The paradigm shift: modern mobile web means higher standards, context-aware design, and leveraging new standards for a seamless, powerful user experience. Don’t just shrink your site. Rethink it. The value is enormous! #PWAs #WebDevelopment

1/🧵 When we pursue big goals, we often overestimate the space of what's truly possible right now—our "adjacent possible." We dream of heroic leaps, but reality is far more constrained (and that's actually empowering, not defeatist!).

2/n It's easy to believe legends succeed with grit alone, yet most bold leaps are lottery tickets—one in 10,000 pays off. Survivorship bias makes us idolize them and ignore quiet failures. As individuals, we can't buy infinite tickets. We have one career, one shot at a time.

3/n Embracing constraints isn't giving up—it unlocks agency. Within our limited adjacent possible, we *choose* what to do next. Every tiny action brings new possibilities within reach, revealing options that weren't visible until we took the last step.

4/n The future is a fog. Don't bet everything on a distant vision with little info—slice actions small, make decisions iteratively. Every new move updates your possibilities and lets you steer toward better outcomes. Optionality compounds over time.

5/n Don't zigzag randomly—set a north star vision. With each micro-decision, bias toward your north star and let compounding steps arc you to outcomes that once looked impossible. The path isn't linear but emerges through steady, small, smart moves.

6/n Judgment is key: Use your brain and your brawn. Sprinting endlessly won't help if you're running the wrong way. Take space to steer. Make sure actions clear the "survival" bar, then bias for moves that unlock long-term value and line up with your vision.

7/n In teams, coordination costs make this much harder! Vision ossifies, debates spiral endlessly. Instead, bias actions to those that are no-brainers NOW and keep iterating until the high-stakes decisions become obvious.

8/n Lower expectations to survive early rounds—quiet releases for self-selected early adopters > big risky launches. Combine existing viable things instead of building everything from scratch; "duct tape" is underrated innovation!

9/n This isn't a call to give up on heroism—it’s a plea to be the tortoise, not always the hare. Fluid strength, embracing uncertainty, iterative value creation—these outcompete rigid heroics over the long game. Be water, my friend.

10/n Greatness doesn't come from a single magic leap, but from smart, steady, iterative progress. Have the humility to admit uncertainty, stay adaptable, and let remarkable results emerge from countless small, aligned actions. #IterativeAdjacentPossible

1/🧵 AI has immense potential for good—but it won’t happen by default. We have to *shape* AI’s impact, accounting for indirect social effects, with a broad, human-centered lens—not just tech or finance.

2/ In this new era, "Coactive Computing" matters—a rare word meaning acting jointly, *together*. AI shouldn't be passive or corporation-centered, but human-scaled, collaborative, prosocial, private, and meaningful.

3/ We must rethink intelligence itself—not as something centralized and threatening, but as a mass noun: emergent, distributed, and flowing like water. No all-powerful dossier—just collective, decentralized intelligence.

4/ Most scaled consumer AIs get trapped in "engagement-maximizing"—reinforcing sycophancy and addictiveness, not what actually benefits people. When models hide context from users, that’s deeply misaligned and manipulative.

5/ My context is *mine*—I should control what an AI remembers, what context is in play, and be able to take it anywhere. Hiding what a model knows is creepy and shows misaligned incentives.

6/ Humans are multifaceted. The idea that a chatbot stores a single memory model on you is wrong. Context must be granular, portable, and under personal control—the opposite of collapse into engagement-farming dossiers.

7/ I want software to bloom for humanity, not serve as a dopamine trap. The future is Private Intelligence: context-rich, working only for me, flowing only on my turf. A hyper-aggregator chasing engagement is the opposite—and terrifying.

8/ If your AI agent isn’t paid for by you, it’s someone else’s agent. Alignment and privacy are only possible if you control the context and the incentives.

9/ Fun and productivity don't have to be opposed—when software aligns our wants with our aspirations, both can thrive. Software's purpose: to *do*, not just to exist.

10/ Infinite personalization creates new echo chambers—LLMs may subtly calibrate what you see, making "balance" subjective. Are we ready for filter bubbles in self-perception?

11/ Platform power matters: app stores, walled gardens, and aggregation models all impact who controls context. Open, trusted, composable systems—not closed, walled platforms—must be our aim for a healthy digital fabric.

12/ Prompt injection, trust, and context privacy are design challenges we *must* solve to make AI a force for good. Building reliable, resonant, coactive systems—where users own their context—is the only sustainable path.

1/ LLMs aren't just AI—they're a leap towards Intelligence Amplification (IA). It's not about replacing humans, but centering and empowering us. If you unplugged the tech, would your system still work, just with more friction? That's a test for true IA. (1/n)

2/ Traditional media is “fossilized”—written for a fixed audience, static and dead. Oral media is alive but loses scale. LLMs let media adapt perfectly to whoever's reading: a new, living, "liquid media" that molds to each person, like a one-on-one conversation. (2/n)

3/ The secret sauce of LLMs? Qualitative nuance at massive, quantitative scale. We used to throw away nuance for scalability. Now, mass adaptation is possible, fundamentally shifting how organizations make sense of themselves and act. Human-style reasoning, everywhere! (3/n)

4/ System 1 LLMs auto-complete; System 2 reason. But what about "System 3"? That's LLMs plugged into society’s collective intelligence—connecting, adapting, and learning not just as one mind, but as an emergent web of countless contributors. (4/n)

5/ Embodiment matters. Human intelligence is deeply tied to a single body—one life, one perspective. LLMs mimic us not by living, but by feasting on the fossilized record of our embodied thought: our writing. But just like fossils, it gives a skewed view of reality. (5/n)

6/ LLMs forget everything after each chat. They don’t have persistent memory like people do. Some memory gives value; some risks privacy or context confusion. The uncanny feeling of “being understood” is a mirage—LLMs are tabula rasa every time. (6/n)

7/ Conversation with LLMs isn't always the right interface. I crave not a "chatbot buddy," but an extension of my agency—a substrate that lets me shape information and act with language directly, like finger-on-glass vs jabbing with a stick. (7/n)

8/ LLMs make any text "executable." That means instructional and contextual text are indistinguishable at a technical level, making injection and prompt attacks lurking risks we don't know how to fully defend against yet. (8/n)

9/ The future isn't lonely islands of AI-powered tools—it's distributed "archipelagos" with bridges. AI-native software should presume interconnectedness, not isolation. Liquid, adaptable, and networked, not brittle and fixed. (9/n)

10/ Cosy Tech is the future: tech at human scale, warm, personal, and empowering—not tech built for mass markets at the expense of individual needs. It should feel grown, not built. Let’s build optimistic, inviting, and trustworthy AI. (10/10)
